meta.description: Staging file for species under research — not yet promoted to spotted or html. Plants and wildlife coexist, distinguished by the 'type' field.
meta.animal_group_audit_note: Corrected animal_group on 13 wildlife entries (Arachnid→Spider, Reptile→Turtle, Insect→specific by family). 11 entries left with unresolved values — no matching THEME_MAP bucket exists for bees/wasps/flies/lacewings/fungi.

quick_hits: The scarlet tubular flowers bloom in step with the spring migration of **Ruby-throated Hummingbirds** moving north along the Gulf Coast.
The brilliant red seeds that follow are beautiful, familiar-looking, and **seriously toxic**.
Thorny, deciduous, and tough enough to grow in pure sand and **salt spray**.
A true **Florida native** that provides year-round value for wildlife.
species.origin: Native to the southeastern United States from North Carolina south through all of Florida and west to Texas, and into northeastern Mexico. Found in coastal hammocks, sandhills, flatwoods, mesic and upland woods, and pine rocklands throughout the state.
more_information: The Coral Bean's spring flowers are almost perfectly timed to coincide with the northward migration of Ruby-throated Hummingbirds along the Florida peninsula — an evolutionary alignment that makes this plant one of the most important early-season hummingbird fuel stops on the Gulf Coast.

The seeds are among the most deceptively dangerous in Florida. They look like polished red beads — and have historically been used exactly that way, strung into jewelry and decorations across the Caribbean and Gulf South. They contain a suite of toxic alkaloids including erysopine, erythrinine, and hypaphorine, known to cause paralysis when ingested. In Mexico the seeds have been used as rat poison. Handle with gloves; keep away from children.

Despite its toxicity, the Cherokee people used root decoctions medicinally for kidney and urinary conditions, and Florida Ethnobotany devotes two full pages to traditional uses of this plant across Indigenous cultures of the Southeast.

The name herbacea — Latin for 'herbaceous' or 'low-growing' — was given because this species is more shrubby than the large tropical Erythrina trees common in South America and Africa, some of which reach 100 feet and are used as shade trees for coffee and cacao plantations. This is the modest North American representative of a globally significant genus.

A member of the Fabaceae family — the same legume family as Sunshine Mimosa, Royal Poinciana, and Dwarf Poinciana also in the park. The genus name Erythrina comes from the Greek erythros, meaning red — a reference to the flowers and seeds that define the plant.
wildlife_value: The Coral Bean is one of the most hummingbird-focused native plants in Florida. Its long scarlet tubular flowers are shaped specifically for hummingbird bills and timed to coincide with Ruby-throated Hummingbird migration in spring — feeding northbound birds at a critical moment in their journey. Butterflies with longer tongues and bumblebees also work the flowers. The brilliant red seeds that follow are dispersed by birds that carry them from the split pods. The plant's thorny stems and dense branching habit make it an ideal refuge for small birds seeking protected roosting and nesting cover. The Erythrina borer moth (Terastia meticulosalis) uses it as a larval host.

blocks.label: Habit
blocks.text: Deciduous to semi-evergreen depending on location. In North and Central Florida typically dies back to the ground in cold winters and resprouts vigorously from a woody caudex and deep taproot in spring.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long terminal racemes 4 to 6 inches or more bearing brilliant scarlet tubular flowers up to 3 inches long, appearing in spring before or as leaves emerge. Flower-bearing stems are visibly smoother and greener than leaf-bearing stems — a useful identification feature in early spring.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Thin, bean-like pods that split at maturity to display bright coral-red seeds. Pods persist through fall and winter providing a long-lasting ornamental display.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: Brilliant coral-red, polished-looking. TOXIC — contain multiple alkaloids causing paralysis. Handle with gloves.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Compound, with three deltoid (arrowhead-shaped) light green leaflets, each with tiny prickles along the underside of the margin. Stems armed with short curved spines.

blocks.label: Bark
blocks.text: Smooth, light gray to whitish on mature stems.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In spring, watch for vivid scarlet flower spikes appearing on nearly bare branches — and watch those branches for hummingbirds. In fall and winter, the split bean pods displaying brilliant red seeds against bare stems are easy to spot. Run a careful finger along a leaflet margin to feel the fine prickles — a reliable tactile ID feature.

size.height: typically 3 to 8 feet in North and Central Florida; up to 20 feet in frost-free South Florida
size.habit: open, rounded crown from spreading irregular branches; often multi-stemmed from a short trunk
size.texture: medium-coarse, open and somewhat airy in appearance

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy · loamy · well-drained · acidic to neutral
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: high — documented in salt marshes and coastal thickets
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: dies back to ground in hard freezes but resprouts reliably; root-hardy to USDA zone 8
growing_conditions.note: can look sparse when not in flower — best interplanted with other shrubs

edibility.detail: Not edible. All parts should be considered toxic. Some traditional uses of boiled leaves exist in limited cultural contexts but these do not make it safe to eat. Seeds are severely toxic.

toxicity.people: All parts contain toxic alkaloids including erysopine, erythrinine, and hypaphorine, known to cause paralysis when ingested. Seeds are the most dangerous part and have been used as rat poison. Keep away from children and pets. Wear gloves when handling seeds or propagating.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. Coral Bean seeds contain alkaloids that can cause weakness, tremors, and paralysis; keep dogs away from fallen seeds.

invasive.notes: Native species. Not invasive.

quick_hits: The velvety red spikes — up to 18 inches long — have **no petals** at all; their color comes from masses of tiny red female flowers.
Native to Southeast Asia and New Guinea, it's been grown as an ornamental so long that its **wild ancestor** is hard to pin down.
The name 'Chenille' is French for **caterpillar** — which the fuzzy hanging spikes resemble.
Every Chenille Plant in the nursery trade is **female** — the species is dioecious, and the plain-flowered males were never brought into cultivation.
species.origin: Native to Southeast Asia, particularly Malaysia and New Guinea. A member of the Euphorbiaceae family — the same family as the Copperleaf, Peregrina, and Pencil Tree also in the park.
more_information: Every Chenille Plant sold in nurseries worldwide is female. The male plants exist in the wild but were never brought into horticulture — their flowers are inconspicuous, so no one wanted them. The flowers are also apetalous (petal-less): instead of bright petals, the plant packs its display into masses of fine red styles, forming those velvety 18-inch spikes — one of the most dramatic flower forms grown in Florida tropical gardens. The Euphorbiaceae family spans extraordinary diversity — from tiny weeds to massive trees, from cactus-like succulents to the rubber tree — many sharing a milky latex sap.
wildlife_value: Limited direct wildlife value. The long red catkins are showy flower spikes rather than a major nectar source, and the plant is wind- and insect-pollinated; the dense foliage offers some cover for small birds. It is grown mainly as an ornamental for the trailing flower tassels.

blocks.label: The Chenille Plant is dioecious
blocks.text: only the female plant produces the notable flowers. All plants available in the horticultural trade are female. Flowers: showy red, drooping in cattail-like, narrow pendant clusters up to 18 inches in length — the signature feature. Flowers year-round with peak flowering during summer. Propagation by cuttings only.

size.height: 4 to 6 feet, older specimens can grow taller with some support. Stems heavily foliated with 6 to 8 inch long, ovate medium-green leaves. Vigorous, upright, coarse-textured shrub. Growth rate: fast.

edibility.detail: Not recommended. Mildly toxic if ingested.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if ingested. Contains irritating sap that can cause skin irritation and gastrointestinal discomfort. Keep away from children and pets. Wear gloves when pruning.
toxicity.dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs and cats. The milky sap can irritate skin and, if the plant is chewed, cause drooling and gastrointestinal upset. Keep pets from chewing it.

invasive.notes: No. Not on any Florida invasive list.

quick_hits: The flowers are orange-red, but the structure that makes this plant striking is the pagoda-like **tiered** arrangement of the flower clusters — each cluster stacked above the last in diminishing rings, like a Buddhist temple tower.
A member of the mint family (Lamiaceae), confirmed by **square stems**. Related to the park's Starburst Bush and Turk's Turban.
Spreads aggressively by **underground suckers**. A single plant expands into a colony quickly. Worth knowing before siting it.
**Hummingbirds** and butterflies work the flowers actively. A useful addition to a pollinator planting.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, and Southeast Asia. Member of Lamiaceae — the mint family.
more_information: The Pagoda Flower earns its name from its flower head, not its individual blooms: dozens of small orange-red tubular flowers are stacked in tiered, pyramid-shaped clusters that rise above the big, dark, broadly lobed leaves like the roofs of a pagoda. The display sits at the branch tips and can be a foot or more tall.

The tiered structure suits pollinators that hover and probe - hummingbirds and butterflies work the tubular flowers, and the plant is a reliable nectar draw through the warm months. It belongs to the genus Clerodendrum, in the mint family (Lamiaceae), alongside the park's Starburst Bush and Turk's Turban.
It spreads enthusiastically by underground suckers, which is its main drawback - left unchecked it forms a thicket, so it is best given room or contained with a barrier.
wildlife_value: Hummingbirds and butterflies are frequent visitors. The tiered flower structure presents accessible nectar at multiple heights. The long bloom season and dense flower production make it a reliable pollinator plant.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Orange-red, tubular, in tiered pagoda-like whorls on upright stalks. The stacked arrangement is the defining visual feature.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Opposite, broadly ovate, medium green with slightly wavy margins.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright shrub, 3 to 6 feet. Spreads by underground suckers to form colonies. Square stems throughout.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The tiered, pagoda-like flower arrangement is distinctive — stacked rings of bloom on an upright stalk. Square stems confirm the mint family.

size.habit: upright, soft-stemmed shrub spreading by underground suckers
size.texture: bold - large lobed leaves and tiered flower heads

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, fertile, well-drained
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender tops, resprouts; USDA zones 9B to 11
growing_conditions.note: spreads aggressively by root suckers - contain with a barrier or pull suckers to keep it in bounds.

edibility.detail: Not edible. No food use documented.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity documented.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Not on the FISC invasive plant list. Spreads by underground suckers — monitor and manage spread into adjoining areas.

species.other_notes: The Pagoda Flower recorded at Palma Sola grew on Welcome Island and was lost in the storms. The park's Tree Crinums have since expanded into that space, as they tend to do. All park observations predate the loss.
species.internal_notes: REMOVED specimen (Welcome Island); storm loss; Tree Crinums took the space. Published as species page for now. Placeholder hero says 'help us photograph this' — mismatch for a gone plant; see §7 gone-plant placeholder decision.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: The climbing relative of the park's King's Mantle — same genus, completely different growth habit. Where King's Mantle stays compact and bushy, Bengal Trumpet climbs vigorously and will cover a fence, pergola, or tree if given the opportunity.
The pale blue flowers — one of the few true blues in the tropical vine palette — are produced in long drooping clusters.
FISC Category II invasive in Florida. Escaped cultivation in several counties and is establishing in natural areas. Educational specimen.
The sheer scale of a mature Bengal Trumpet in bloom is impressive — but so is its ability to overwhelm supporting structures and adjacent plants.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to India and Bangladesh. Member of Acanthaceae — the acanthus family. Same genus as King's Mantle (Thunbergia erecta) and Black-eyed Susan Vine (T. alata).
more_information: Bengal Trumpet is the climbing relative of the park's King's Mantle - the same genus, Thunbergia, but a completely different plant in habit. Where King's Mantle stays a compact shrub, this is a vigorous twining vine that will cover a fence, pergola, or tree, carrying long drooping clusters of pale blue, trumpet-shaped flowers with a yellow throat. True blue is rare in the tropical-vine palette, and that color is the draw.

It is also a cautionary specimen. Below ground it builds a massive tuberous root system - reported up to about 70 kg (150 pounds) - that resprouts vigorously when the top is cut, which is exactly why it is so hard to remove and why Florida lists it as a Category II invasive. Cutting the vine down does not kill it; the tubers send it right back.
The park keeps a single managed plant on the nursery fence as an educational example - lovely to look at, and instructive about how an ornamental becomes a problem.
wildlife_value: Bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds visit the flowers. The long bloom season and abundant flower production make it a significant nectar source.
As a vigorous climber it creates dense canopy structure when established on a support — providing shade and habitat but also potentially overwhelming host plants.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Pale blue to lavender with a yellow to white throat, trumpet-shaped, in hanging clusters (racemes). Up to 3 inches across. Produced over a long season.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Heart-shaped to triangular, medium green, on twining stems.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Vigorous twining vine, 20 to 30 feet or more. Woody with age. Not compact — this plant covers large areas.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The pale blue trumpets in drooping clusters are distinctive — few Florida vines offer this color. Compare with King's Mantle: same genus, completely different scale and habit.

size.height_length: a vigorous twining vine, 20 to 30 feet or more on a support
size.spread: wide-spreading; covers fences, pergolas, and trees
size.habit: woody, twining evergreen vine; builds a large underground tuber
size.texture: bold - large heart-shaped leaves and drooping flower clusters

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, fertile, well-drained
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender tops, resprouts from the tuber; USDA zones 9 to 11
growing_conditions.note: a FISC Category II invasive - needs strong support, hard containment, and prompt removal of any escaping shoots; cutting back alone will not control it.

edibility.detail: Not an edible plant.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity documented.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: FISC Category II invasive in Florida. Escaped cultivation in Broward and Miami-Dade counties and is establishing in natural areas. Do not plant near natural areas. Kept here as an educational specimen. Remove any volunteers that establish outside the planting area. Below ground it builds a tuberous root system that can reach about 70 kg (150 pounds). Cutting the vine back does little, since it resprouts from the tubers, which is why it is difficult to remove once established.

species.other_notes: Grows along the fence between the park nursery and the adjacent water facility, near the back gate, climbing the fence line. Known around the park as Sky Vine.

quick_hits: An aquatic or semi-aquatic native creeping plant that forms dense mats at pond edges and in moist soil. The round penny-shaped leaves are arranged in whorls around the stem — the source of both common names.
A Florida native and part of the naturalized pond edge community. Provides ground cover and surface structure for small aquatic organisms and frogs.
Not a weed in the sense of being unwanted — at pond edges where it naturally belongs, it performs useful ecological functions. Primarily documented here as part of the pond edge flora.

To every homeowner in Bradenton trying to maintain a uniform lawn, this plant is better known as Dollarweed — and it is the enemy. It only thrives in constantly saturated soil, making it a living diagnostic tool: if Dollarweed is taking over a lawn, the irrigation system is running too long or there is a drainage problem.
species.origin: Native to eastern North America including Florida. Found in freshwater marshes, pond edges, and moist disturbed areas. Member of Araliaceae — the ivy and ginseng family.
more_information: Dollarweed gets its name from its leaves: round, glossy, and coin-like, each held up on a central stalk like a tiny green umbrella - the feature that separates it from look-alike lawn weeds, whose leaf stalks attach at the edge. It is a native creeping plant of pond edges, marshes, and wet ground, spreading by slender rhizomes into low mats.

It is often cursed as a lawn weed, but that is mostly a sign of the conditions it likes: chronically wet or over-irrigated ground. At a pond edge, where it belongs, it is a useful native that stabilizes wet soil. At Palma Sola it grows along the moist margins where pond meets land.

The plant is technically edible, with a peppery bite, but harvesting from lawns or roadsides is discouraged - it readily absorbs whatever is in the water and soil, including lawn fertilizer and runoff.
wildlife_value: The dense low mats at the water's edge provide cover for insects, amphibians, and other small shoreline creatures that larger wildlife feed on. The tiny flowers draw small pollinators. Its main role is as shoreline groundcover and soil stabilizer in wet ground.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, white to greenish, in small rounded clusters on short stalks.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Round, glossy, scalloped at the edge, 1 to 2 inches across, with the stalk attached at the center of the leaf (peltate) - the key ID feature.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low, creeping, mat-forming; spreads by rhizomes at and just below the surface.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Round, coin-like leaves on central stalks forming a low mat in wet ground or at the pond edge. The center-attached leaf stalk separates it from similar weeds.

size.habit: creeping, mat-forming aquatic to semi-aquatic perennial

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wet to moist; pond edges, marshes, and over-irrigated ground; tolerates shallow standing water
growing_conditions.note: thrives where soil stays wet; in lawns its presence usually signals overwatering or poor drainage.

edibility.detail: Technically edible but strongly discouraged from urban harvest. Acts like a sponge — readily absorbs lawn fertilizers, pesticides, and urban runoff. Leave it in place for its shoreline-stabilizing value.

toxicity.people: Not toxic to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: A small non-native groundcover, naturalized throughout Florida in disturbed areas, lawns, and roadsides. The tiny white star-shaped flowers cluster in tight whorls where the leaves meet the stem and feed small native bees and other insects.

Despite being non-native, it has become one of the most reliable nectar sources for the introduced Larra bicolor wasp, a biological-control insect used against pest mole crickets - which is why some Florida programs actually value it.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to tropical South America, Central America, and the Caribbean. Naturalized across southern Florida. Member of Rubiaceae — the coffee family.
more_information: Shrubby False Buttonweed is a small, scrappy member of the coffee family (Rubiaceae) that turns up across Florida lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground. It is non-native - from tropical America - and naturalized statewide: low and many-branched, with tiny white star-shaped flowers gathered in tight clusters where the leaves meet the stem.

Its claim to fame is an insect partnership. The introduced Larra bicolor wasp, brought to Florida as a biological control against destructive non-native mole crickets, feeds heavily on its nectar; plantings of this weed and its relatives are used to support the wasp. So a plant most people would pull quietly underwrites one of Florida's pest-control success stories.
It is recorded here as part of documenting everything growing on the grounds, weeds included - and this one earns its keep.
wildlife_value: A useful nectar plant for small native bees and other insects, and notably for the introduced Larra bicolor wasp, a biological control agent against pest mole crickets - the wasp depends on small-flowered nectar sources like this one. As a non-native it offers little as a larval host for native insects.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, white, star-shaped, in tight clusters (whorls) at the leaf nodes along the stem.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small dry capsules; reproduces by seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Narrow, opposite, rough-textured, often with clusters of smaller leaves in the axils.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low, many-branched, weakly woody-based groundcover.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Whorls of tiny white flowers tucked where the narrow leaves meet the stem, low in disturbed ground. Look for small bees and wasps working the flowers.

size.habit: low, branching, weakly woody-based perennial groundcover

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy, disturbed soils; tolerates poor ground
growing_conditions.note: a naturalized non-native weed; reseeds freely.

edibility.detail: Non-toxic. Not a food plant.

toxicity.people: Not toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Non-native, naturalized from tropical America. Not on Florida's FISC invasive list. A common weed of lawns and disturbed ground; valued in some programs as a nectar plant for the introduced Larra bicolor wasp, a biological control for pest mole crickets.

quick_hits: Opens at four in the afternoon. Closes the next morning. The name is precise — it is one of the more reliable plant clocks in horticulture, triggered by declining light intensity rather than temperature.

Native to Peru, where it was cultivated by the Aztecs and Incas as an ornamental and medicinal plant. Introduced to Europe by Spanish explorers in the 16th century — one of the first American ornamentals to reach European gardens.
The seeds and roots contain trigonelline and other alkaloids — mildly toxic. The plant has a long history of use as a purgative in traditional medicine.
The flowers are strongly fragrant in the evening — adapted for nocturnal moth pollination.

A living genetics lesson: Four-o-clocks display incomplete dominance. Cross a red-flowered plant with a white one and the offspring are pink — not red, not white. A single bush can carry yellow, pink, and white flowers simultaneously, and individual petals sometimes split two colors right down the middle. Gregor Mendel never worked with these.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Peru. Cultivated by the Aztecs and Incas; introduced to Europe and then globally by Spanish traders in the 16th century. Member of Nyctaginaceae — the four-o'clock family, same family as bougainvillea.
more_information: The Four-o'clock is named for its habit, not its looks: the trumpet-shaped flowers open in late afternoon, around four o'clock, stay open through the night, and close by the next morning. The timing is a pollination strategy - the evening fragrance and pale, glowing flower colors are aimed at night-flying moths, especially hawkmoths.

A single plant can carry flowers of different colors at once - pink, red, yellow, white, or streaked combinations - because the pigment genes sort out unpredictably, which is part of why Mirabilis was used in early studies of inheritance. It grows from a large tuberous root that lets it return year after year and ride out dry spells.

Native to Peru and cultivated since pre-Columbian times, it spread worldwide as an easy ornamental. It is a member of Nyctaginaceae, the four-o'clock family that gives the park's bougainvilleas their name. It self-seeds readily but is not an invasive concern here.
wildlife_value: Moths are the primary pollinators — attracted by the evening fragrance and the pale flower colors that reflect low light. Hawk moths and sphinx moths are the main visitors.

Because the flowers open in late afternoon and close by morning, daytime pollinators largely miss them. The primary visitors are hawk moths and sphinx moths — Florida's nocturnal pollinators — guided by the pale trumpet-shaped flowers and the sweet evening fragrance.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Trumpet-shaped, in pink, red, yellow, white, or bi-color. Open late afternoon, close mid-morning. Fragrant in the evening.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, dark, angular achene-like fruits. Each flower produces one.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Opposite, ovate, smooth, medium green.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Bushy perennial, 2 to 4 feet tall. Dies back to a large tuberous root in cold weather; resprouts in spring.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Visit in the morning and find the flowers tightly furled into tubes; return after 4 PM and find them fully open and fragrant. That daily timing is the easiest confirmation. Look for trumpet flowers in mixed colors — pink, red, yellow, white, often on the same plant — on a bushy, 2-to-4-foot perennial.

size.habit: bushy herbaceous perennial from a tuberous root

edibility.detail: Not an edible plant. Sometimes used as a food coloring in traditional South American cooking but not safe to eat directly.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. Seeds and roots contain alkaloids. Ingestion may cause nausea and vomiting. Not severely toxic under normal conditions but avoid ingestion.
toxicity.dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs. Seeds contain alkaloids — ingestion may cause vomiting and stomach upset. Roots most concentrated. Keep dogs from digging the roots.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Can self-seed in disturbed areas but not a significant invasive concern.

quick_hits: The burgundy-red leaves are the display here — a deep, wine-red color unusual in the tropical foliage palette and striking against green neighbors.

Despite the common name, it is not a true hibiscus. It is in the same genus (Hibiscus) but is more closely related to roselle — the species whose calyces make hibiscus tea — than to the ornamental hibiscus. The leaves can be used the same way: tart, cranberry-flavored, and edible raw in salads.
The flowers are small and pink — less showy than the ornamental hibiscus relatives — but produced continuously through warm weather.
Not reliably cold-hardy; may die back in frost but regrows quickly from roots.

The deeply lobed burgundy leaves can stop people in their tracks — and occasionally cause a double-take. The leaf shape has been known to prompt questions from visitors unfamiliar with ornamental hibiscus.

At the park, these grow behind the office and galleria along the open field edge and in Hibiscus Island, reaching 10 to 15 feet before the annual hard cut. The cutback never fails to horrify onlookers. They come back every time. Fear not.
species.origin: Non-native. Likely native to tropical Africa or Asia, though exact origin is uncertain. Cultivated throughout the tropics as an ornamental and food plant. Member of Malvaceae — the mallow family.
more_information: Cranberry Hibiscus is grown for its foliage rather than its flowers. The deep wine-red, maple-shaped leaves give a burgundy accent uncommon in the tropical palette, holding their color through the warm season. The pale pink to rose flowers are small and open briefly, mostly late in the day.

The leaves are edible - tart and faintly cranberry-flavored, used raw in salads or cooked, and sometimes steeped for a rosy drink. It should not be confused with True Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa), the species whose fleshy red calyces make hibiscus tea; here it is the leaves, not the calyces, that are used.

Easy and fast in Florida heat, it behaves as a short-lived perennial in Bradenton and is often replaced from cuttings, which root readily. It earns its place as a foliage accent and an edible-garden curiosity.
wildlife_value: Bees and butterflies visit the flowers. The dense foliage provides cover. Hummingbirds occasionally visit.
The edible leaves and the continuous flower production make it a dual-purpose plant in a wildlife garden — food for people and nectar for pollinators.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, pale pink to rose, five-petaled. Produced continuously on new growth. Less showy than ornamental hibiscus relatives.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Deeply lobed, burgundy-red throughout, with a metallic sheen. The color is the definitive identification feature.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright to sprawling shrub, 4 to 8 feet in warm conditions. May die back in frost; regrows from roots.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The deep burgundy-red lobed leaves are unmistakable — no other common Florida shrub has this color with this leaf shape. The small pink flowers are secondary.

size.habit: upright, branching, short-lived perennial shrub (often grown as an annual)

edibility.detail: Leaves edible — tart, cranberry-flavored, used raw in salads or cooked. Note: do not confuse with True Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) whose fleshy calyces make hibiscus tea. H. acetosella leaves are the edible part here, not the calyx. Flowers also edible.

toxicity.people: Not toxic to people. Leaves consumed as food.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Not flagged by UF/IFAS.

quick_hits: The same two-lobed leaf as its close relative the Orchid Tree (Bauhinia variegata) nearby — use the leaf to find it, then the flower to tell them apart.
Only one fertile stamen per flower — unusual in the legume family, where multiple stamens are the norm. The species name monandra means single-stamened.

The flowers open white or pale pink and age to deep pink with a distinctive yellow and red blotch on the upper petal. A single tree displays multiple colors simultaneously as flowers age at different rates.
UF/IFAS rates it as having high invasive potential in Florida — not yet on the FISC list but worth monitoring.

Find it in the southeast corner of the park, past the bridge from the fruit garden, on the bank of the large pond — among trees that students and staff protected with riprap against the pond's expanding edge.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Myanmar and introduced widely across the tropics. Member of Fabaceae — the legume family. Also known as Napoleon's Plume from its introduction to France during the Napoleonic era.
more_information: The Pink Orchid Tree sits near the Orchid Tree (Bauhinia variegata), and the two are worth comparing directly. Both have the same unmistakable two-lobed leaf. The flower differences are the key: B. monandra has a single fertile stamen (visible as the one stamen with an anther); B. variegata has multiple. The flower color progression of B. monandra — opening pale and aging to deep pink with a yellow-and-red blotch — is also distinctive, giving a single tree a multi-tone appearance when flowers of different ages are open simultaneously.

The species name monandra means single-stamened in Greek — the botanists who named it considered the single stamen the plant's defining characteristic, and they were right that it is unusual. The legume family norm is ten stamens; Bauhinia already reduced that considerably; this species reduced it to one.

The park's Pink Orchid Tree grows in the southeast corner — cross the bridge from the fruit garden and turn right, following the bank of the large pond. This stretch of bank is one Randy and student volunteers fortified with riprap to protect against the pond's widening edges eroding the soil beneath the trees. It was also the site of a gorgeous Red Silk Cotton Tree that was lost to the storms; a new small replacement has since been planted there.

The observation that confirmed this tree's presence in the collection was made by iNaturalist contributor billieknight on May 22, 2025 — a beautiful close-up of the multi-colored blooms in full display. Randy has not yet seen it in person. It is worth finding. It may be the most tucked-away Feature plant in the entire collection.
wildlife_value: Butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds visit the flowers. Like its close relative, it blooms in late winter to early spring, providing nectar at a time of relative pollinator scarcity.
Seed pods produced abundantly — explosive at maturity, similar to B. variegata. Birds feed on seeds.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Open white to pale pink, age to deeper pink. Upper petal has a distinctive yellow and red blotch. Only one fertile stamen visible with an anther — this is the key species-level ID feature. Up to 4 inches across.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Flat, woody legume pods similar to B. variegata. Twist and split explosively.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Two-lobed, identical in form to B. variegata. The leaf alone does not distinguish the two species — use the flower stamen count.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Semi-deciduous small tree, typically 15 to 25 feet.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The two-lobed leaf confirms genus. To identify species, count stamens on an open flower: one fertile stamen with a visible anther = B. monandra (this plant). Multiple stamens = B. variegata (nearby). The color progression on aging flowers — pale to deep pink with yellow-red blotch — is also distinctive.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range, well-drained
growing_conditions.note: self-seeds; UF/IFAS rates it high invasive potential, so remove volunteer seedlings.

edibility.detail: Flowers and young leaves edible in some cultures. Not a food plant in Florida landscape use.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Not currently on Florida's FISC invasive list, but UF/IFAS rates it as having high invasive potential in Florida. Self-seeds abundantly via explosive pods. Monitor volunteers and remove promptly from natural areas.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: A small Asian composite weed — one of the smallest-flowered plants in the park, naturalized in disturbed soil and path edges. The tiny yellow flowers on thin branching stems are easy to overlook.
Documented here as part of the complete honest inventory. Not a collection plant and not a feature — simply present as a naturalized volunteer in disturbed areas of the park grounds.
You'll usually spot these tiny underdogs along the compacted edges of walking paths or tucked into mulch beds after a summer rain. Our volunteers mostly leave them alone unless they get too rowdy.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Asia. Naturalized widely in disturbed areas throughout the warm United States, including Florida. Member of Asteraceae — the daisy family.
more_information: Oriental False Hawksbeard is a small Asian member of the daisy family that has naturalized as a common weed of lawns, path edges, and disturbed ground across the warm-climate United States, including all of Florida. It is among the smallest-flowered plants documented in the park, included here for completeness - part of recording everything that actually grows on the grounds, not only the planted specimens.

The plant begins as a flat basal rosette of lobed leaves, then sends up slender wiry stems topped by tiny yellow flower heads that look like miniature dandelions. Each head produces small dry one-seeded fruits tipped with fine white bristles that carry on the wind, which is how it turns up in new disturbed spots. It completes its short life cycle quickly and reseeds freely, especially in cool, moist months.
wildlife_value: Minor wildlife value. The small flower heads draw tiny native bees and flies, and the seed is taken by some small birds. Its main ecological role here is as a quick-cycling colonizer of bare and disturbed ground.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny yellow composite heads, like miniature dandelions, on slender branching stems.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small dry one-seeded fruits (achenes) tipped with fine white bristles, wind-dispersed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: In a flat basal rosette, lobed (lyre-shaped), with a larger end lobe.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low rosette sending up thin wiry flowering stems, mostly under about 2 feet.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A small rosette weed in disturbed soil and path edges, with thin stems of tiny yellow dandelion-like flowers. Easy to overlook - one of the smallest-flowered plants on the grounds.

size.height_length: flowering stems mostly 6 inches to 2 feet
size.spread: a small basal rosette a few inches across
size.habit: low annual to short-lived rosette herb with wiry flowering stems

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: any disturbed soil; common in lawns, beds, and path edges
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: dies back and reseeds; most visible in cool, moist months; USDA zones 8 to 11
growing_conditions.note: a spontaneous naturalized weed, not planted; reseeds freely.

edibility.detail: Not edible.

toxicity.people: Not toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive list. A common naturalized weed in disturbed areas. Not a management priority.

quick_hits: The drooping yellow flowers are the source of ylang-ylang oil — one of the most important ingredients in perfume, including the original Chanel No. 5.
The scent is richest at night, which is when the tree releases its heaviest fragrance.
The name comes from a Tagalog word often translated as "flower of flowers."
The curly, ribbon-like petals look more like dangling sea creatures than typical blossoms.
species.origin: Native to the Philippines, Indonesia, and surrounding tropical Asia and the western Pacific. Cultivated across the tropics for its perfume flowers and grown in frost-free Florida.
more_information: Ylang-ylang is grown the world over for one reason: its fragrance. The fresh flowers are steam-distilled into an essential oil prized in perfumery for a rich, sweet, slightly fruity scent, and it remains a backbone note in countless perfumes — most famously the original Chanel No. 5.

The tree concentrates its fragrance for the night, when the smell becomes most intense, a strategy aimed at night-flying moth pollinators. Its flowers are as odd as they are aromatic: long, narrow, curling yellow-green petals that hang in ribbony clusters and look almost like drooping starfish.
The common name comes to us from Tagalog and is often translated as "flower of flowers," a fitting tag for a tree grown purely for its perfume.
wildlife_value: The fragrant night-scented flowers attract moths and other nocturnal pollinators. The tree's fruit clusters feed birds, and the canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing evergreen tree with somewhat drooping, brittle branches.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Drooping clusters of long, narrow, curling petals, green aging to deep yellow; intensely fragrant, especially at night.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Clusters of small green berries ripening to near-black.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, oval, pointed, with wavy margins.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Hanging clusters of curly, ribbon-like yellow flowers — and the scent, which is the real signature. Crush nothing; just stand near a flowering tree at dusk.

size.height: 30 to 50 feet (often kept lower in cultivation)

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained, rich
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; branches are brittle and can break in wind

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown for its perfume flowers, the source of ylang-ylang essential oil.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity from the growing plant. The concentrated essential oil can irritate or sensitize skin in some people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The plant is not known to be harmful, though concentrated oils should be kept away from pets.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

quick_hits: The popular houseplant with a braided trunk is this tree — the braiding is done by hand on young, flexible saplings.
In the wild it is no tabletop plant: it grows into a substantial tree with showy, brush-like flowers.
It is wrapped in good-luck lore, said in feng shui to attract prosperity — hence "money tree."
Its large nuts are edible roasted, tasting something like chestnuts or peanuts.
species.origin: Native to wetlands and riverbanks of Central and South America, from Mexico to northern South America. Widely grown as an ornamental and houseplant, and outdoors in frost-free Florida.
more_information: Most people meet the money tree as a small braided houseplant, and the braid is no accident of nature — growers weave the supple young trunks together by hand, and the plant grows on with the braid locked in. Left to itself outdoors, it becomes a real tree with a swollen-based trunk suited to its wet native haunts.

In flower it is striking: large buds split to release long, brush-like sprays of cream stamens, followed by big woody seed pods. Inside those pods are the nuts, which are edible roasted and reportedly taste of chestnut or peanut — a genuine if little-used food.
The "money" name comes from feng shui tradition, where the plant is associated with prosperity and good fortune, which is much of why it became such a ubiquitous gift plant.
wildlife_value: The large nectar-rich, brush-like flowers attract bats, moths, and other pollinators in the tropics. The seeds and pods feed wildlife where it grows naturally.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium evergreen tree with a green, often swollen-based trunk; shrubby and braided in cultivation.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large buds opening to long, showy brushes of cream-white stamens.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Large, woody, oval seed pods containing several rounded nuts.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Palmately compound, with five to nine glossy leaflets spreading like fingers from the leaf stalk.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Hand-shaped (palmate) leaves of five or more leaflets and, in cultivation, the distinctive braided trunk. Flowering specimens show big brush-like cream blooms.

size.height: 30 to 60 feet in the ground (much smaller potted)

growing_conditions.light: full sun to bright shade (tolerates indoor light)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · rich, moist; tolerates wet ground
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; happiest with steady moisture

edibility.detail: The roasted nuts are edible and reportedly chestnut- or peanut-like. Not a common food plant here.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs — Pachira aquatica (money tree) is listed as non-toxic by ASPCA.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368058024 (mariaaaz); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review. Genus-level ID — refine to species when possible.

quick_hits: In summer it bursts into clouds of crinkled, crepe-paper flowers — the source of both its beauty and its name.
Mature trees shed their bark in patches, leaving a smooth, mottled, almost muscular trunk.
The bloom runs for weeks, and trimming spent flowers can coax a second flush.
It is one of the most planted flowering trees in the South — for good reason.
species.origin: Native to East and Southeast Asia, especially China and Korea. Introduced to the southern United States in the 1700s and now one of the most widely planted ornamental flowering trees across the warm South, including Florida.
more_information: Crape-myrtles earn their place in southern gardens with a long, lavish summer bloom. The flowers have a distinctive crinkled, crepe-paper texture — the source of the name — and come in white, pink, red, lavender, and purple, often carried for many weeks.

The show does not end with the flowers. As the trees mature, the outer bark flakes away in patches to reveal a smooth, sinuous, mottled trunk in shades of tan, gray, and cinnamon — a feature as prized in winter as the flowers are in summer.
The park's specimen is identified at the genus level (Lagerstroemia); the many garden crape-myrtles are mostly hybrids, and a firm species name will be added once it can be confirmed.

A local note: please resist "crape murder" — the harsh topping-back to knuckled stubs seen across so many Florida yards is unnecessary, and it spoils both the tree's natural vase shape and the beautiful peeling bark.
wildlife_value: The pollen-rich flowers are heavily worked by bees and other pollinators through the summer. Birds eat the small seed capsules in fall and winter, and the branching canopy offers perches and cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A deciduous large shrub or small multi-trunked tree with an open, vase-shaped crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Crinkled, crepe-paper-textured blooms in dense clusters at branch tips, summer into fall; white to pink, red, or purple.

blocks.label: Bark
blocks.text: Flakes in patches on older wood, leaving a smooth, mottled tan-and-gray trunk.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Small, oval, glossy, often coloring orange-red before dropping.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Crinkled summer flower clusters and a smooth, peeling, mottled trunk. The crepe-paper petal texture is the clincher.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: powdery mildew can affect older varieties in humidity; good air flow helps

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. Considered non-toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs — crape-myrtle is listed as non-toxic by ASPCA.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

quick_hits: Despite the name, it is neither a pine nor a palm — its leaves spiral up the stem like the thread of a screw.
Many species stand on a cage of stilt-like prop roots that lift the trunk clear off the ground.
Those aerial roots brace the plant against wind and shifting, often coastal, soils.
Some screwpines bear big, knobby fruit that looks startlingly like a green pineapple or a brain.
species.origin: Native to the Old World tropics — coastal and island habitats across the Pacific, Southeast Asia, Africa, and Australia. Various species are grown ornamentally in frost-free regions including Florida.
more_information: Screwpines (Pandanus) are unmistakable once you know them: long, strap-shaped, often spiny-edged leaves arranged in a tight spiral that climbs the stem like screw threads — the source of the common name, even though the plant is unrelated to pines.

Many species are propped up on a tepee of stout aerial "stilt roots" that grow down from the trunk into the soil, bracing the plant against wind and erosion in the exposed coastal and island habitats where it often lives. The effect is of a plant standing on its own scaffolding.

The genus is also useful: some species produce large, segmented fruits (resembling a knobby pineapple) that are eaten in the Pacific, and the fragrant leaves of one relative, pandan, are a beloved Southeast Asian flavoring. The park's plant is identified to genus; a species name will follow once confirmed.
wildlife_value: The fruit of fruiting species feeds birds, bats, and other animals, and the dense, spiny rosettes provide protected nesting and shelter. The prop-root tangles offer cover for small creatures.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A palm-like evergreen shrub or tree, often branched, frequently raised on stilt roots.

blocks.label: Roots
blocks.text: Stout aerial prop roots descending from the trunk to the ground in many species.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, strap-shaped, leathery, spiraling up the stem, usually with spiny margins and midribs.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: In fruiting species, a large, round to oval, segmented head resembling a knobby pineapple.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Spiraling strap-leaves and, often, a basket of stilt roots holding the trunk off the ground. The spiral arrangement is the signature.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · tolerant of poor and salty coastal soils
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; leaf margins can be sharply spiny

edibility.detail: Varies by species. Some screwpines have edible fruit (Pacific species) or fragrant flavoring leaves (pandan); others are ornamental only.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity reported. The spiny leaf margins are the main hazard.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful, though the spiny leaves can cause injury.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368019600 (randall_carter); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review. Genus-level ID — refine to species when possible.

quick_hits: The dazzling color is not the flower — it comes from papery modified leaves called bracts, surrounding tiny true flowers.
The actual flowers are small, white, and tucked in threes inside each splash of color.
It blooms hardest when stressed: lean, dry conditions trigger the heaviest color.
Watch the stems — they are armed with sharp, hooked thorns.
species.origin: Native to South America, especially Brazil, Peru, and Argentina. Now one of the most widely planted tropical ornamental vines worldwide, thriving in warm, sunny Florida.
more_information: Bougainvillea's famous color is a trick of the leaves. The vivid magenta, orange, purple, red, or white "petals" are actually bracts — modified leaves — arranged in threes, each cradling a small, tubular, cream-white true flower at its center. The bracts last far longer than ordinary petals, which is why a plant can blaze with color for months.

It is also a plant that rewards a little hardship. Bougainvillea blooms most heavily in full sun and lean, somewhat dry conditions; pampered with rich soil and too much water, it tends to grow lush and green with fewer bracts. That preference suits Florida's sunny, sandy sites well.

The trade-off is the thorns. Vigorous, scrambling, and armed with stout hooked spines, bougainvillea climbs and sprawls readily and needs space and respect when pruned. The park's plant is identified at the genus level.

There is a remarkable backstory to the name. Bougainvillea was collected on the first French voyage around the world (1766–1769) and named for its commander, Louis-Antoine de Bougainville — but the person who most likely gathered it was Jeanne Baret, the expedition's botanical assistant. She had joined disguised as a man, because women were barred from French naval ships, and in doing so became the first woman known to circumnavigate the globe.
wildlife_value: The bracts and small flowers attract butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds. The dense, thorny tangle provides excellent protected nesting and shelter cover for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A vigorous, scrambling, thorny evergreen vine or mounding shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, white, tubular true flowers in threes, each surrounded by showy papery bracts in magenta, purple, red, orange, or white.

blocks.label: Stems
blocks.text: Woody, arching, armed with sharp hooked thorns.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Oval, pointed, medium green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Masses of papery color on a thorny, climbing plant. Look closely for the small white flowers inside each set of three bracts.

size.height: 15 to 30 feet as a climber (kept smaller as a shrub)

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: high — and lean, dry conditions boost bloom
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; sharp thorns — site away from paths

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: Low toxicity. The sap can cause mild skin irritation, and the thorns can cause puncture wounds, but it is not poisonous to eat.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The main hazard is mechanical — the sharp thorns, not poison.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

quick_hits: The dense, rounded clusters of small tubular flowers give it the nickname "flame of the woods."
Each bloom is a slender tube — perfectly shaped for the butterflies that probe them for nectar.
It flowers in flushes much of the year in Florida's warm climate.
In parts of Asia the flowers carry religious and ceremonial meaning.
species.origin: Native to tropical Asia, with the most common ornamental forms tracing to India and Sri Lanka. Now a staple flowering shrub across the tropics and a mainstay of warm-climate Florida landscapes.
more_information: Ixora is grown for its flowers — tight, rounded clusters of small, four-petaled tubular blooms in fiery red, orange, pink, or yellow, set against glossy evergreen leaves. The massed effect gives it common names like "flame of the woods," and in Florida's warmth it blooms in repeated flushes through much of the year.
The long, narrow flower tubes are a clue to its pollinators: butterflies and other long-tongued insects can reach the nectar at the base, making a healthy ixora hedge a reliable butterfly stop.

The plant carries cultural weight in its homeland as well, where the flowers feature in Hindu worship and ceremony. The park's plant is identified at the genus level; the species or hybrid will be added once confirmed.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich tubular flowers are a steady draw for butterflies and other pollinators. The dense evergreen shrub provides good cover and some nesting habitat for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A dense, rounded evergreen shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tight, rounded clusters of small, four-petaled, long-tubed flowers in red, orange, pink, or yellow; blooming in flushes much of the year.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, opposite, oblong, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Domed clusters of small tubular flowers on a tidy, glossy-leaved shrub. The dense "bouquet" flower heads are characteristic.

growing_conditions.light: full sun to part sun (best bloom in sun)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic preferred · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers acidic soil — leaves yellow in alkaline conditions

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people reported.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

quick_hits: Scratch a leaf and the mark stays for the life of that leaf — which is how the tree earned its name.
The leaves are so thick and leathery that people once used them as playing cards and writing paper.
It often starts life as an epiphyte, sprouting in another tree's crook before sending roots down to the ground.
The sticky sap was historically used as a crude glue and even to caulk boats — but it is an irritant, so look, do not carve.
species.origin: Native to the Caribbean, southern Florida, and tropical Central and South America. Grown as an ornamental in frost-free regions and naturalized in parts of South Florida.
more_information: The autograph tree's claim to fame is its leaves: thick, stiff, and leathery enough that a fingernail or stick leaves a permanent etched scar, which darkens and persists as long as the leaf lives. People have signed them, used them as note paper, and even dealt them as makeshift playing cards.

It has an unusual way of getting started, too. Seeds often sprout high in the crotch of another tree as an epiphyte, then send long roots down to the ground — a strangler-fig-like strategy that lets it begin life above the competition.

A word of caution behind the novelty: the plant's milky-yellow sap (latex) is sticky and irritating, historically pressed into service as a glue and boat caulk. It is best admired, not carved — and the showy white-and-pink flowers and round green fruit are reason enough to leave the leaves alone.
wildlife_value: The flowers attract bees and other pollinators. The fruit splits to reveal seeds in red pulp that birds eat and disperse, and the dense evergreen canopy offers cover and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: An evergreen shrub or small tree, sometimes starting as an epiphyte with dangling roots.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy, white with a pink or yellow center, somewhat lotus-like.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, green, leathery; splits open to show seeds in bright red pulp.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Very thick, stiff, leathery, paddle-shaped, dark glossy green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Thick, leathery paddle leaves — the obvious test is a light scratch, which leaves a lasting mark. Round green fruit and lotus-like white flowers confirm it.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · limestone · salt-tolerant · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; tough and adaptable in warm coastal sites

edibility.detail: Not edible. The fruit and sap are not eaten.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. The sticky latex sap is irritating to skin and eyes, and the fruit and seeds are not for eating.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. The irritant latex sap and the fruit can cause mouth irritation and stomach upset in dogs.

invasive.notes: Native to extreme South Florida but can spread from cultivation; not on Florida's main invasive lists, though plant thoughtfully in warm regions.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370312846 (sethtlayne); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: One plant, three flower colors at once: blooms open deep purple, fade to lavender, then white over three days — yesterday, today, and tomorrow.
The shifting colors mean every flowering bush looks tie-dyed in purple and white.
The flowers are sweetly fragrant, especially in the evening.
It is genuinely poisonous — the berries are dangerous, and it is a well-documented cause of seizures in dogs.
species.origin: Native to tropical South America, especially Brazil. Widely grown as a fragrant flowering ornamental in warm climates, including Florida.
more_information: The charm of Brunfelsia is in the timing. Each flower opens a rich purple, fades to pale lavender the next day, and turns nearly white the day after — so a single bush in full bloom carries all three colors at once, the basis for the playful name "yesterday, today, and tomorrow." The flowers are sweetly, sometimes intensely fragrant, especially toward evening.

Behind the pretty face is real toxicity. The plant contains brunfelsamidine and related compounds, and all parts — leaves, flowers, and especially the berry-like fruit — are poisonous. It is a particularly well-documented hazard for dogs: the fruits seem to attract them, and ingestion can cause tremors and seizures, sometimes severe.
It is a relative of nightshades and petunias, a family famous for mixing beauty, food, and poison in roughly equal measure.
wildlife_value: The fragrant flowers attract butterflies, moths, and other pollinators. Wildlife value is otherwise limited by the plant's toxicity, and the berries are not a safe food source for pets.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A rounded evergreen shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Flat, five-lobed blooms that open purple and fade through lavender to white over about three days; fragrant.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, berry-like, toxic.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, oblong, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A shrub carrying purple, lavender, and white flowers all at the same time — the color shift is unmistakable. Note any berries and keep pets away from them.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic preferred · well-drained, rich
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers acidic, moist but well-drained soil

edibility.detail: Not edible — poisonous. No part should be eaten; the berry-like fruit is especially dangerous.

toxicity.people: Toxic if ingested. Contains brunfelsamidine and related toxins that can cause tremors, seizures, and other serious effects. All parts are poisonous; do not eat any part.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs and well documented as a cause of seizures. The berries seem to attract dogs and are especially dangerous; ingestion can cause tremors and convulsions (ASPCA lists Brunfelsia as toxic). Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #143174191 (teschaim); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review. Genus-level ID — refine to species when possible.

quick_hits: The crisp white iris-like flowers earn it the name fortnight lily — they tend to bloom in rhythmic flushes, roughly every two weeks.
Each individual bloom lasts only a day or two, but the plant keeps producing them over a long season.
Despite the name, it is not a true lily — it is a member of the iris family.
It grows from tough, grassy, fan-shaped clumps that shrug off heat, drought, and neglect.
species.origin: Native to southern and eastern Africa. Widely planted as a tough, low-care ornamental in warm climates around the world, including throughout Florida.
more_information: Fortnight lily is grown for being beautiful and nearly indestructible. It forms dense, upright clumps of stiff, grassy, fan-shaped leaves, out of which rise wiry stems carrying flat, six-parted flowers — white marked with yellow and violet — that look very much like a small iris, which is exactly what the plant is related to.

The common name comes from its bloom rhythm: flowers tend to appear in waves, often spaced about a fortnight (two weeks) apart, with each bloom lasting only a day or two but the show continuing over many months. The same flower stems are reused for years, so old stalks are best left uncut.
Its toughness is the selling point — it handles heat, drought, poor soil, and salt with little complaint, which is why it shows up in so many low-maintenance landscapes.
wildlife_value: The iris-like flowers attract bees and other pollinators. The dense, grassy clumps provide ground-level cover and shelter for small animals and insects.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A clumping, evergreen, iris-relative perennial with fans of stiff, grassy leaves.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Flat, six-parted, iris-like, white marked with yellow and violet; each lasts a day or two, in flushes.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, narrow, stiff, sword-shaped, in flat fans.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads by rhizome into expanding clumps; also sets seed.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Fans of stiff, grassy leaves topped with small white iris-like flowers blooming in waves. The flat iris-form flower is the giveaway.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerates poor soil and brief drought
growing_conditions.note: very low-maintenance; leave old flower stems uncut, as they rebloom

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: Low toxicity. Iris-family plants can cause mild stomach upset if eaten, but it is not seriously poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Iris-family plants (including the rhizomes) can cause mild vomiting or drooling in dogs if chewed; keep pets from grazing on it.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

quick_hits: A Florida-native wildflower whose slender scarlet (sometimes pink or white) blooms are built for hummingbirds.
The tubular flowers fit a hummingbird's bill and tongue almost exactly — a partnership in plain sight.
It is a tireless self-seeder, popping up year after year and filling in gaps in a sunny bed.
A true sage (Salvia), it carries the family's faintly aromatic foliage.
species.origin: Native to the southeastern United States, including Florida, and south into Mexico and tropical America. A common wildflower of open woods, clearings, and disturbed ground, and a favorite in native and pollinator gardens.
more_information: Tropical sage is one of Florida's most rewarding native wildflowers for wildlife. Its loose spikes of slender, tubular flowers — usually vivid scarlet, sometimes pink, coral, or white — are shaped and colored for hummingbirds, which probe them for nectar, and they draw butterflies and bees as well.

It is also delightfully easy. The plant blooms over a long season, sets seed freely, and reseeds itself year after year, drifting through a sunny bed and filling in bare ground without becoming a nuisance. That self-sowing habit makes it a low-effort anchor for a pollinator planting.
As a true Salvia — the sage genus — it carries the family's lightly aromatic foliage, a soft herbal scent when the leaves are brushed.
wildlife_value: A top-tier pollinator plant: the tubular red flowers are a magnet for hummingbirds — especially the ruby-throated hummingbird, which migrates through Manatee County in spring and fall — and also feed butterflies and bees over a long bloom season. As a native that reseeds readily, it provides reliable, season-long nectar with little effort.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A soft-stemmed, upright native perennial wildflower, often grown as an annual.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Loose spikes of slender, tubular, two-lipped flowers, usually scarlet (also pink, coral, or white), much of the year.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Triangular to heart-shaped, softly hairy, faintly aromatic.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Self-seeds freely, returning and spreading year to year.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Airy spikes of slender red tubular flowers on a soft, upright plant in a sunny spot, often with a hummingbird working the blooms.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: reseeds readily — let it self-sow or deadhead to control spread

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown as a native ornamental and pollinator wildflower.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. Considered non-toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Tropical sage is not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive — it reseeds but stays well-behaved.

quick_hits: A Florida-native waterlily whose fragrant white flowers float open on the pond surface — and follow the sun.
The blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon, repeating the cycle for several days.
That sweet fragrance is real and gives the plant its species name, odorata.
Many parts — buds, seeds, and roots — were traditional foods for Native peoples.
species.origin: Native to ponds, lakes, and slow waters across much of North America, including Florida. A common and characteristic plant of still freshwater throughout the state.
more_information: The American white waterlily is a true Florida native and one of the most recognizable plants of still freshwater: round, notched, floating leaves (lily pads) and fragrant, many-petaled white flowers resting on the surface, each with a sunburst of golden stamens at the center.

The flowers keep a daily schedule. They open in the morning, close again in the early afternoon, and repeat for several days before sinking below the surface to set seed underwater. The sweet scent — the reason for the name odorata — is strongest on freshly opened blooms.

Beyond beauty, it is a plant of genuine use and ecological weight. Its flower buds, seeds, and starchy rhizomes were eaten by Native peoples, and in the pond it shelters fish and invertebrates beneath its pads while feeding a range of wildlife above and below the water.
wildlife_value: The pads shade and shelter fish, frogs, and aquatic insects, while the flowers feed bees, beetles, and other pollinators. Seeds and foliage are eaten by waterfowl, and the whole stand supports a rich pond community above and below the surface.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A rooted aquatic perennial with floating leaves and flowers, growing from a submerged rhizome.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Fragrant, many-petaled, white (sometimes pink-tinged) with golden centers; floating, opening mornings.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Round, leathery, green above and often purple beneath, with a single notch (the classic lily pad).

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads by rhizome and seed; flowers are pulled underwater to ripen seed.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Notched, round floating pads and fragrant white surface flowers on a still pond. The morning-opening, afternoon-closing rhythm is part of the show.

size.spread: pads and flowers spreading across the water surface from rhizomes
size.water_depth: roots in mud under 1 to 5 feet of water

growing_conditions.water: still or slow fresh water, 1 to 5 feet deep, rooted in muck
growing_conditions.note: a native aquatic for ponds and pond margins; spreads by rhizome

edibility.detail: Edible historically — the flower buds, seeds, and starchy rhizomes were eaten by Native peoples. Not a casual food today.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people from the traditionally eaten parts.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive — a valued native aquatic (though it can spread vigorously in a small pond).

quick_hits: The little white-and-yellow daisy that pops up everywhere is one of Florida's most valuable nectar plants for bees and butterflies.
Its seeds are the flat black "sticktights" that hitchhike home on your socks and your dog.
Despite its weedy reputation, the young leaves and flowers are edible — used as a cooked green and herbal tea.
It blooms nearly year-round, feeding pollinators when little else is flowering.
species.origin: Native to Florida and the warm Americas. An abundant wildflower of roadsides, fields, clearings, and disturbed ground throughout the state, often dismissed as a weed.
more_information: White beggarticks — "Spanish needles" to many — is one of those plants whose weedy reputation hides real worth. Its small white-rayed, yellow-centered daisies bloom nearly year-round, and they are a powerhouse nectar and pollen source. Florida studies repeatedly rank it among the very top plants for honeybees, native bees, and butterflies, precisely because it flowers when so little else does.

The nickname comes from the seeds: flat, narrow, black, and tipped with tiny barbs that latch onto fur, feathers, and clothing — the familiar "sticktights" or "beggar's ticks" you pick off after a walk. It is the plant's highly effective hitchhiking strategy for getting its seeds moved around.

It is also a genuine wild edible: the young leaves and flowers are eaten as a cooked green and steeped as an herbal tea, with a long history of folk medicinal use. Weed, wildflower, pollinator buffet, and potherb all at once.
wildlife_value: One of Florida's most important nectar and pollen plants — consistently ranked among the top sources for honeybees, native bees, and butterflies, and a larval host for several butterflies and moths, including the dainty sulphur. It feeds pollinators nearly year-round, including in lean seasons when little else blooms.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing, branching native annual or short-lived perennial wildflower.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small daisies with white rays and yellow centers, blooming nearly year-round.

blocks.label: Fruit/Seeds
blocks.text: Flat, narrow, black seeds tipped with barbs ("sticktights") that cling to fur and clothing.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Toothed, often divided into leaflets, medium green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Small white-and-yellow daisies on a weedy, branching plant — and the barbed black seeds that stick to your clothes are the unmistakable confirmation.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerates poor and disturbed soil
growing_conditions.note: reseeds aggressively — welcome for pollinators, but pull where you do not want it

edibility.detail: Edible — the young leaves and flowers are eaten as a cooked green and steeped as an herbal tea. A genuine wild edible.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. Young leaves and flowers are eaten; long folk-medicinal history.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive (though it is weedy and reseeds freely). A top pollinator plant often left as a 'good weed.'

quick_hits: A handsome vine with glossy leaves and large funnel-shaped lavender-pink flowers — and a serious dark side.
Every part runs with milky sap that is highly poisonous, packed with heart-stopping toxins.
It is one of the world's worst invasive weeds, smothering native vegetation where it escapes.
The park keeps it as a teaching example: beautiful, useful-looking, and exactly the kind of plant not to turn loose.
species.origin: Native to Madagascar. Introduced widely as an ornamental and rubber-source plant, it has become a damaging invasive weed in parts of Australia, the Pacific, and elsewhere, and is treated as a plant of concern where it escapes.
more_information: Madagascar rubbervine is a cautionary tale wrapped in an attractive package. The vine is genuinely handsome — glossy paired leaves and large, funnel-shaped flowers in soft lavender-pink — and it was spread around the warm world as an ornamental and as a potential rubber source, thanks to its abundant latex.

That latex is the problem on two fronts. First, it is highly toxic: the milky sap contains cardiac glycosides — heart-affecting poisons — and all parts of the plant are dangerous to people and livestock if eaten, with the sap also an irritant. Second, the vine is aggressively invasive where it escapes, climbing over and smothering native trees and shrubs; in parts of Australia, rubber vines (this species together with its close relative Cryptostegia grandiflora, which are easily confused and hybridize) became some of the most serious environmental weeds on the continent.
The park keeps it deliberately, as a teaching plant — a vivid lesson that "pretty and useful" can still mean "poisonous and invasive," and a reason to think hard before planting an unfamiliar exotic.
wildlife_value: Wildlife value is low and outweighed by its hazards. The flowers offer some nectar, but the plant's toxicity and its tendency to smother native vegetation make it a net negative for local wildlife.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A vigorous woody, climbing or scrambling evergreen vine with milky sap.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large, funnel-shaped, lavender-pink (sometimes paler), in clusters.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Paired, horn-like pods that split to release silky-tufted seeds carried on the wind.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, dark green, oval, opposite, exuding white latex when broken.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A glossy-leaved climbing vine with funnel-shaped lavender-pink flowers and milky sap. Paired horn-like seed pods confirm it — and signal a plant to control, not spread.

size.height: climbs 10 to 30 feet into supporting vegetation

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerant of poor soils
growing_conditions.note: aggressive and toxic — kept here only as a managed teaching specimen

edibility.detail: Not edible — poisonous. No part should be eaten.

toxicity.people: Highly toxic if ingested, and the milky sap irritates skin and eyes. Contains cardiac glycosides that affect the heart. All parts are dangerous; do not eat any part and avoid the sap.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. The cardiac glycosides in the sap and foliage can cause vomiting, heart rhythm problems, and serious illness. Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: A recognized invasive of serious concern — one of the world's worst environmental weeds where it escapes (notoriously in Australia). Grown here only as a contained teaching example; not a plant to propagate or release.
