quick_hits: A sprawling shrub that lights up in fall with bright yellow flowers — handsome, and a recognized Florida invasive.
It blooms around the holidays, which is how it earned the name Christmas senna.
Long, dangling bean pods follow the flowers, scattering seeds that sprout readily and let it spread.
Like its relatives, it is a butterfly host — sulphur caterpillars feed on it — which complicates an otherwise weedy reputation.
species.origin: Native to South America. Introduced as an ornamental for its bright fall bloom, it has escaped cultivation and naturalized in Florida, where it is listed as an invasive species.
more_information: Christmas senna is a fast, sprawling, semi-woody shrub grown for its showy display of bright yellow, cup-shaped flowers, which peak in fall and early winter — the source of the holiday name. After flowering come long, hanging, cylindrical seed pods typical of the senna group.

Those seeds are the catch. They germinate readily, and the plant has escaped gardens to naturalize across Florida, where it is recognized as an invasive (FISC Category II) that crowds into natural and disturbed areas. The park includes it partly as a teaching example of an attractive ornamental that does not stay put.

There is a genuine ecological twist, though: like other sennas and cassias, it serves as a larval host for sulphur butterflies, whose caterpillars feed on the foliage — so even a plant being managed as a weed can carry real value for pollinators, a nuance worth posting honestly.
wildlife_value: A larval host for sulphur butterflies (such as cloudless and orange-barred sulphurs), whose caterpillars feed on the leaves, and the flowers offer nectar and pollen to bees. That butterfly-host value is set against its status as an invasive that displaces native plants.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing, sprawling, semi-woody shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Bright yellow, cup-shaped, in clusters, peaking in fall and early winter.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Long, hanging, cylindrical pods containing many readily sprouting seeds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with several pairs of oval leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A loose, sprawling shrub covered in bright yellow flowers in fall, followed by long dangling pods. The pods plus the autumn bloom mark it — and flag a plant that spreads.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerant of poor and disturbed soils
growing_conditions.note: spreads readily by seed — manage to prevent escape into natural areas

edibility.detail: Not edible. The pods and seeds are not for eating.

toxicity.people: Mild toxicity. The pods and seeds have a laxative (cathartic) effect and can cause stomach upset if eaten; not a serious poison but not a food.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Senna species can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs (cathartic effect); keep pets from eating the pods and seeds.

invasive.notes: Listed as an invasive in Florida (FISC Category II). It escapes cultivation and naturalizes; grown here partly as a teaching example. Manage to prevent spread; do not plant where it can escape.

safety_note: Petioles are smooth and spineless, so handling the fronds carries less risk than with many other palms. The fruit is not a food source for people, but it is not toxic. No known toxicity to people or dogs — otherwise a harmless plant.
quick_hits: This is essentially Florida's Cabbage Palm (Sabal palmetto) scaled up — same silhouette, same costapalmate fronds, but with a trunk nearly twice as thick and a crown that can span 20 to 25 feet across.
The species name domingensis means 'of Santo Domingo' — the old colonial name for the entire island of Hispaniola. It was formally described by the Italian botanist Odoardo Beccari in 1908.

Botanists have noted that Sabal domingensis, the closely related Puerto Rico Hat Palm (S. causiarum), and the Cuban S. maritima may all be forms of a single species — the main thing telling them apart is fruit shape and size.

The fronds hang characteristic fibrous threads from the notches between leaf segments — white, curling filaments that persist throughout the life of the leaf and give the crown a slightly shaggy texture up close.
origin: Native to Hispaniola — the island shared by Haiti and the Dominican Republic — and also present in Cuba. In the wild it grows in secondary vegetation and open savanna habitats from about 330 to 3,300 feet above sea level.

It is not native to Florida or anywhere in the continental United States, but it has been grown as an ornamental in South Florida for some time, valued for the same qualities that make the native Cabbage Palm a landscape workhorse — toughness, wind resistance, and an unhurried dignity — in a larger package.
more_information: Sabal domingensis belongs to a tight cluster of large Caribbean palmettos that includes the Puerto Rico Hat Palm (Sabal causiarum) and the Cuban Sabal maritima. The three are similar enough that some palm taxonomists have argued they may all be one species; for now, botanists distinguish them mainly by fruit.
The Hispaniola Palmetto's fruit is distinctly pear-shaped (pyriform) and larger than the other two, measuring roughly half an inch across.

Like all members of the Sabal genus, this palm has costapalmate fronds — fan-shaped, but with a stiff central rib (the costa) that angles the blade rather than leaving it flat. It is also what palm growers call a 'tillering' palm with saxophone-style root architecture, meaning it grows a distinct underground heel.
Planting it with the top third of that heel above soil level is important for long-term health.

On Hispaniola the palm has been useful for generations. The leaves are harvested for thatching and woven into hats, baskets, and mats. That practical relationship with the plant is built right into one of its Haitian Creole names — latanier-chapeau, meaning 'hat palm.'
wildlife_value: The spring inflorescences — long, branched, arching stalks carrying clusters of small creamy white flowers — are a reliable draw for bees and other insects. Across the Sabal genus, flowers are insect-pollinated, primarily by bees attracted to the fragrant blooms.

The small pear-shaped black drupes that follow are nutritious and taken by birds — a pattern well documented for Sabal palms across the region, with local birds including cardinals, blue jays, fish crows, and mockingbirds regularly consuming the fruit. The dense crown also provides roosting and shelter cover, particularly for cavity-nesting and perching birds.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small creamy white flowers appear in spring on long, branched inflorescences that arch outward from within the crown. The stalks are typically about 3 feet long and held among the leaves rather than extending beyond them. Flowers are insect-pollinated, primarily by bees.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Pear-shaped (pyriform) black drupes about 0.4 to 0.6 inches in diameter, each containing a single seed. Fruit size and shape are the main character that distinguishes this species from its close Caribbean relatives. Fruits are dispersed by birds.

blocks.label: Fronds
blocks.text: Costapalmate — fan-shaped but with a stiff central rib extending from the petiole into the blade, giving the frond a three-dimensional bow rather than a flat disc. The crown holds 20 to 30 fronds, each with about 90 leaflets. Thin, curling fibrous threads hang from the notches between segments and tend to persist throughout the life of the leaf. Petioles are smooth — no spines or teeth.

blocks.label: Trunk
blocks.text: Solitary, massive, and upright, reaching up to 60 feet in cultivation and about 2 feet in diameter. The upper trunk is typically cloaked in interlaced old leaf bases; these eventually drop to reveal smooth, closely ringed scars on a gray-brown shaft. Young plants can flower before any visible trunk has formed.

blocks.label: Propagation
blocks.text: By seed only — no suckering or clonal spread. Seeds may take several months to germinate.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Year-Round: Take in the sheer bulk of the trunk — far stouter than Florida's native Cabbage Palm, its lower length ringed with the scars of fallen fronds. Step closer and look up into the crown for the white, curling fibrous threads hanging from the notches between leaf segments; they are subtle but distinctive. Spring: Watch for the long, arching flower stalks — about 3 feet of branched creamy white clusters tucked within the canopy, drawing a steady buzz of bees. Summer to Fall: Look up for the heavy clusters of small black pear-shaped fruits; if birds are working the crown, that is most likely why.

seasonality.bloom_description: Arching, branched inflorescences of small creamy white flowers emerge from within the crown of leaves.
seasonality.notes: Bloom and fruiting timing are extrapolated from the Sabal genus broadly; verify against the park's specimen for local timing.

size.height: up to 60 feet in cultivation (Palmpedia); some sources cite the wild maximum near 53 feet)
size.growth_rate: slow; approximately 1 foot per year once established

growing_conditions.light: full sun; tolerates part shade but thrives in full sun
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: adaptable to most well-drained soils including sand and loam; prefers light sandy soils; avoid consistently waterlogged conditions
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: good once established; tolerates hot, dry inland conditions
growing_conditions.spacing: allow at least 15 feet from structures given the eventual crown spread of 20 to 25 feet

other_notes: The IUCN Red List (2023) classifies Sabal domingensis as Least Concern globally, noting that it occurs in gardens, scientific reserves, and forest reserves and has a stable population.

Specimens grown in Florida are sometimes labeled or sold as Sabal causiarum (Puerto Rico Hat Palm), and the two look nearly identical without fruit. If the park has documentation of confirmed identity, that is worth noting at the site.

species.watch_invasive_notes: UF/IFAS Assessment status: caution, may be recommended but managed to prevent escape in Central and South Florida zones; not considered a problem species in North Florida. No FISC (Florida Invasive Species Council) Category I or II listing was found. Birds disperse seeds from fruit, which can lead to establishment beyond cultivated areas.
safety_note: The seeds — two to five per fruit, large and smooth — contain cyanogenic glycosides, including amygdalin, the same compound found in apple seeds and peach pits. The concentration is low enough that accidental swallowing of a seed whole poses little real risk, but chewing multiple seeds could cause problems for people and dogs alike.

Spit them out, and keep an eye on curious dogs around fallen fruit. The ASPCA does not list loquat as toxic to dogs, but the seeds and leaves are the parts to watch. The ripe fruit flesh is safe and genuinely delicious — just eat around the seeds.
quick_hits: The species name japonica — 'of Japan' — is a geographical misdirection. Loquat is native to China; it was simply so long-established in Japanese cultivation by the time European botanists encountered it that they assumed Japan was home.

Loquat is one of the very few fruiting trees that flowers in fall and winter in Florida, filling a gap when almost nothing else is in bloom. The fragrant white panicles open October through February — right when most pollinators are hungry for a nectar source.
It belongs to the rose family, which makes it a closer relative of apples, pears, and peaches than of any tropical fruit. The fuzzy skin and clustered fruit are the family resemblance made visible.
Among the 20 or so species in the genus Eriobotrya, this is the only one grown commercially for its fruit anywhere in the world.
origin: Loquat is native to the cooler hill regions of south-central China, where it has been cultivated for well over two thousand years. It was introduced to Japan more than a millennium ago and bred extensively there — which is how European botanists first encountered it, and why the species name japonica stuck even though China is the true homeland.

The western world got its first botanical account from Engelbert Kaempfer in 1690, and by 1784 living plants had reached the National Garden in Paris; specimens followed to the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew in 1787. The tree spread quickly through Mediterranean gardens and into the Americas.

In the United States it was commercially offered by a Philadelphia nursery as early as 1824. UF/IFAS records its arrival in Florida before 1887, and it has been a familiar dooryard fruit tree here ever since.
more_information: The genus name Eriobotrya comes from the Greek erion, meaning woolly, and botrys, a cluster of grapes — a reference to the bunches of downy, fuzz-covered fruit. The description fits: ripe loquats hang in loose clusters, each fruit covered in a soft, apricot-colored down.

Loquat is a genuine multi-season tree. The bold, deeply veined leaves — up to a foot long, dark green above and rusty-woolly beneath — give it a tropical presence year-round. Fragrant flower panicles open in fall and carry through winter, and the bright yellow-orange fruit ripens in late winter to early spring, just as most other fruiting trees are barely leafing out.

The bark is worth a look on older specimens: gray-brown and somewhat patchy, it exfoliates in irregular pieces to reveal a warmer inner layer — a quiet ornamental detail that is easy to walk past but rewarding if you stop. The drooping branch habit adds to the relaxed, informal character of a mature tree.
wildlife_value: Because loquat flowers in fall and winter — a season when nectar is scarce in Florida landscapes — its fragrant white panicles draw a reliable crowd of pollinators. Bees, including honey bees and bumble bees, are the primary visitors; hoverflies (syrphids) are also frequent.
The blooms are self-compatible, meaning a single tree can set fruit, but cross-pollination from a neighboring tree improves yield.

Once fruit ripens in late winter and early spring, birds and squirrels move in quickly. The sweet, juicy flesh is an easy food source at a lean time of year. Because birds disperse the seeds, seedlings occasionally turn up beyond cultivated areas — which is why UF/IFAS recommends managing the tree to prevent escape in central and south Florida.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Creamy white, five-petaled, and fragrant — produced in large terminal panicles covered in a rusty-woolly fuzz. Panicles can hold many individual flowers and are showy enough to notice from a distance. Blooms appear from October through February in Florida.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Pear-shaped to round, yellow to apricot-orange when ripe, and covered in a soft fuzz. Fruits hang in clusters and ripen late winter to early spring. Each fruit holds two to five large, smooth brown seeds. The flesh is sweet, juicy, and edible; the seeds are not.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Alternate, simple, and very large — 8 to 12 inches long — with a leathery, deeply veined surface. Upper surface is dark, glossy green; the underside is coated in rusty-brown woolly hairs. Edges are toothed. New leaves emerge pale and upright, then deepen and droop as they mature.

blocks.label: Trunk and Bark
blocks.text: Typically a single trunk with drooping branches forming a dense, rounded canopy. Bark is gray-brown, somewhat patchy, and exfoliating. The branch tips tend to droop, giving mature trees a relaxed, informal silhouette.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: From a distance, look for the dense, dark rounded canopy and the large, bold leaves — they stand out as unusually tropical-looking for a temperate fruit tree. Get closer and turn a leaf over: the rusty-woolly underside is one of the clearest field marks on the whole plant. In fall and winter, the woolly flower panicles at the branch tips are hard to miss, and the fragrance will reach you before your eyes do. In late winter, scan for clusters of fuzzy yellow-orange fruit hanging from the branch tips.

seasonality.bloom_description: Large, woolly terminal panicles of small, creamy white, fragrant five-petaled flowers. Among the few trees blooming in Florida through the winter months.
seasonality.fruiting_months: late winter to early spring (typically February through April in Florida)
seasonality.notes: Flowers and developing fruit are vulnerable to hard freezes. Fruit set is reliable in USDA zones 8b through 11; it rarely sets fruit further north. The rusty-woolly new growth is visible year-round as the tree continually pushes fresh foliage.

size.height: 15 feet typical in full sun; can reach 25 to 30 feet in shade (UF/IFAS)
size.habit: single trunk, dense rounded to vase-shaped canopy with drooping branches

growing_conditions.light: full sun preferred; tolerates partial shade, though fruit production decreases
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: adaptable — sand, loam, and clay are all acceptable; tolerates high pH; well-drained soil is essential, as root rot occurs in wet or waterlogged conditions
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: moderate to good once established; needs regular water until established, then tolerates periodic drought; fruit production benefits from consistent moisture
growing_conditions.spacing: allow 15 to 25 feet; canopy spread can match or exceed height at maturity

other_notes: Loquat is a fine example of a plant that arrived in Florida as a practical fruit tree and stayed for its ornamental value too. UF/IFAS lists it as suitable for use as a street tree, espalier, patio specimen, and screen — unusual versatility for a tree that also feeds you.

Several ornamental cultivars are available in Florida, including 'Coppertone' (grown primarily for its bronze new growth, rarely fruits) and 'Variegata' (variegated foliage). These are ornamental selections rather than fruiting cultivars; for reliable fruit, the straight species or named fruiting varieties are the better choice.

quick_hits: The flesh of a ripe canistel has the dense, dry, creamy texture of a hard-boiled egg yolk — which is exactly where both its common names come from. It is sweet rather than savory, and closer in flavor to sweet potato pie or baked pumpkin than anything else in the fruit bowl.
Unlike most fruits, canistel continues to ripen after you pick it — but timing the harvest is tricky. Fruit picked too firm may never soften properly and stays astringent no matter how long you wait.

Despite producing a showy, golden fruit, this tree gets almost no help distributing its seeds: insects and birds largely avoid the fruit, apparently put off by its astringency. Humans are the primary dispersal agents.

Florida may hold more diversity of canistel cultivars — named varieties selected for size, sweetness, and shape — than any other location in the world, thanks to decades of grower experimentation in South Florida.
origin: Canistel is native to the lowland forests of southern Mexico, Belize, Guatemala, and El Salvador. From that Central American core it spread — mostly through cultivation — across the Caribbean, and later to Southeast Asia, East Africa, and Australia.

UF/IFAS records its introduction to Florida in the early 20th century. It took root most readily in the warm, frost-free counties of South Florida and the Keys, where it has been a quiet staple of tropical fruit collections and dooryard gardens ever since.
more_information: The Sapotaceae family has a long history of producing economically important tropical fruits, and canistel sits comfortably in that company. Its closest relatives here in the park include mamey sapote and sapodilla — all share the family's characteristic milky latex and glossy foliage.
The bark of canistel is brown and furrowed, and the trunk oozes a sticky white sap when cut, which can gum up pruning tools.

Harvesting canistel well takes practice. The fruit is climacteric — it keeps ripening after you pick it — but fruit taken from the tree too early may remain dry, mealy, and unpleasantly astringent no matter how long it sits. The signal to harvest is when the skin turns from green to yellow-orange.
After that, let it sit at room temperature until it gives slightly to the touch, at which point the flesh becomes creamy and sweet.

Florida growers have developed a range of named cultivars over the decades — Oro, Bruce, Trompo, and Fairchild No. 1 among them — each selected for improvements in fruit size, shape, or flavor. Grafted trees are strongly preferred over seedlings: seedling trees may take three to six years or more to bear fruit, and the results are unpredictable.
wildlife_value: Canistel flowers are pollinated by insects, and the small, fragrant, cream-colored blossoms can attract generalist bees. The tree's wildlife value beyond pollination is limited: the fruit, despite its vivid golden color, is largely avoided by birds and insects, apparently due to its astringent properties. This makes humans the tree's primary seed dispersers in cultivation.

The evergreen canopy does provide shelter and perching habitat for birds year-round, and the dense, glossy foliage contributes to garden-level structural diversity. But visitors hoping to see frugivorous birds working the canopy are unlikely to find much activity here — the canistel is an outlier among tropical fruit trees in offering relatively little to local wildlife.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, cream-colored, and fragrant — produced singly or in small clusters along the branches. They have a silky texture and are bisexual. Insect-pollinated.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Highly variable in shape — round, oval, spindle-shaped, or obovate, often with a pointed tip. Skin is thin, waxy, and smooth; green when immature, turning bright yellow to orange-yellow at maturity. Fruit range from roughly 3 to 5 inches long.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: One to four large, smooth, glossy brown seeds per fruit. Seeds lose viability quickly and should be planted within days of removal from the fruit.

blocks.label: Propagation
blocks.text: Grafted trees are standard in Florida cultivation — they fruit sooner and produce predictably. Seedling trees may take three to six years to bear, with variable results. Air-layering and cleft grafting are also used.

blocks.label: Leaves and Bark
blocks.text: Leaves are evergreen, lanceolate to obovate, thin, and glossy — clustered toward the branch tips. Bark is brown and wrinkled; the trunk and branches exude a white, sticky latex when cut.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Scan the branch tips for the clustered, glossy leaves — they tend to droop slightly and gather toward the ends of the branches. In fruit, the golden-yellow to orange skin is unmistakable and catches the light. Look closely at the stem end of the fruit: a small, persistent calyx is often visible there. The bark is brown and furrowed, and a small nick will reveal the white latex beneath. Flowers are easy to miss — small, cream-colored, and tucked among the leaves.

seasonality.bloom_description: Small, cream-colored, fragrant flowers borne singly or in small clusters along the branches.
seasonality.fruiting_months: Primarily November through March in most of Florida; some cultivars and locations fruit August through October
seasonality.notes: A welcome winter-season producer when most other tropical fruits are out of season. Fruiting timing varies somewhat by cultivar and microclimate.

size.height: 20 to 35 feet in cultivation; can reach 60 to 100 feet in the wild under ideal conditions
size.habit: erect, with a slender to moderately spreading crown

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy · limestone · clay · tolerant of poor soils; prefers well-drained conditions
growing_conditions.spacing: 20 to 25 feet from other trees; can be kept smaller with annual pruning

other_notes: Canistel appears on Miami-Dade County's local invasive and prohibited plant list, though it is not currently listed as a FISC (formerly FLEPPC) Category I or II invasive at the statewide level. Its escape from cultivation into natural areas has been documented in extreme South Florida. It is not a concern in Manatee County's climate.

The botanical name on the park's tag — Lucuma campechiana — is the currently accepted name per Kew's Plants of the World Online, following the reinstatement of the genus Lucuma. Pouteria campechiana remains widely used in horticultural literature and by UF/IFAS; both names refer to the same plant.
safety_note: The ripe fruit is perfectly safe to eat and quite nutritious — rich in beta-carotene, niacin, and potassium. Unripe fruit is astringent and unpleasant but not dangerous. The tree's milky latex sap can be a mild skin irritant for sensitive individuals, so wear gloves when pruning. Otherwise the tree is harmless to people and pets.

safety_note: Keep dogs away from fallen nuts. Macadamia nuts are toxic to dogs — even a small amount can cause weakness in the rear legs, vomiting, tremors, and fever, typically within 12 hours of ingestion. The toxic principle is not yet identified, but the effect is well-documented by the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center.

Most dogs recover within 48 hours with veterinary care, but any suspected ingestion warrants a call to your vet. The nuts are not known to cause toxicity in people or cats; for people, they are safe and delicious, raw or roasted.
quick_hits: The macadamia shell is famously one of the hardest of any nut — it takes roughly 300 pounds of force per square inch to crack one open. Standard nutcrackers don't stand a chance; commercial processors use dedicated husking machinery.

The genus was named by botanist Ferdinand von Mueller in honor of John Macadam (1827–1865), a Scottish-born chemist and physician working in Melbourne, Australia — someone Mueller admired personally, not a famous politician or explorer. Macadam never grew a macadamia in his life.

Despite being Australia's most celebrated nut crop, the commercial macadamia industry was born in Hawaii, not Australia. Trees arrived there around 1881 as ornamental windbreaks, and serious commercial production only got underway in the 1920s.

In the wild, all four Macadamia species are considered rare or threatened — the subtropical rainforest patches they call home in Queensland and New South Wales are critically endangered ecosystems, leaving the wild populations of the world's most beloved gourmet nut surprisingly fragile.
origin: The Macadamia Tree is native to the subtropical rainforests of southeast Queensland and the extreme adjacent corner of northern New South Wales, Australia — a relatively small and now threatened slice of the continent's coastal lowlands. It is one of four Macadamia species, all of them Australian endemics, and the only one commercially dominant worldwide.

Aboriginal Australians harvested macadamia nuts for thousands of years before European contact. Western botany caught up in the late 1850s, when botanists Walter Hill and Ferdinand von Mueller formally described the genus. Mueller named it after his Melbourne colleague John Macadam in 1857.

The tree reached Hawaii around 1881, brought by plant collector W. H. Purvis, initially planted as ornamental windbreaks among sugarcane fields with no particular nut-production ambitions. Commercial cultivation took hold from the 1920s onward, and Hawaii went on to define macadamia for the world.
In Florida, the tree has been grown as both an ornamental and a small-scale crop, and UF/IFAS has been actively exploring its potential as an alternative commercial crop for the state.
more_information: Macadamia integrifolia belongs to the Proteaceae, an ancient plant family that traces its origins to the supercontinent Gondwana. Its closest relatives include banksias, grevilleas, and proteas — the ornamental showstoppers of the Southern Hemisphere — making the macadamia something of a quiet achiever in a very flamboyant family.

The species epithet integrifolia comes from the Latin integer (entire) and folium (leaf), a reference to the leaf margins, which lack teeth on mature foliage. The genus Macadamia was named by Ferdinand von Mueller after John Macadam (1827–1865), a Scottish-born chemist and physician who worked in Melbourne, Australia, and whose work Mueller admired.
Macadam died young, at 38, before the nut bearing his name became famous.

In the wild, Macadamia integrifolia is listed as Vulnerable under Australia's Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act — a striking situation for a tree that is one of the most widely cultivated nut crops on Earth.

Habitat loss and fragmentation of the lowland subtropical rainforests it calls home have left wild populations surprisingly small and scattered, even as commercial orchards thrive across five continents.

Florida's production season runs roughly July through November, which differs from the winter-to-spring harvest typical in Hawaii. The state's UF/IFAS extension services have positioned macadamia as a promising alternative crop for growers diversifying away from citrus, and several small commercial groves are already operating here.
wildlife_value: When macadamia flowers in winter and spring, its long, pendulous white racemes produce abundant nectar and attract a busy crowd of pollinators — honeybees in particular. Bees are considered the primary pollinators; the fragrant blooms have been described as smelling of honey and oranges, which may explain the enthusiasm.
Cross-pollination between nearby trees improves nut set, so a flowering macadamia in a garden or park setting can be a reliable local resource for bees throughout the blooming period.

As a non-native species with no co-evolutionary history with Florida's native fauna, the macadamia does not serve as a larval host for any local butterflies or moths. Its wildlife contribution here is essentially pollinator support during bloom, and the nuts that fall to the ground may attract opportunistic foragers, though the shell's extreme hardness limits which animals can access the kernel.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Slender, drooping racemes 6 to 12 inches long carry dozens of small, fragrant, creamy-white flowers in late winter to spring. The tree is hermaphroditic — each flower has both male and female parts — but cross-pollination between individuals generally improves nut set and quality. Bees are the primary pollinators.

blocks.label: Fruit and Nut
blocks.text: What looks like the nut is botanically a seed enclosed in an extraordinarily hard shell, which is in turn wrapped in a green fibrous husk. The husk splits open as the seed matures and the nut drops. The smooth, shiny inner shell of M. integrifolia requires roughly 300 pounds per square inch to crack — more force than any standard nutcracker can deliver. Inside is a nearly spherical, creamy-white kernel prized for its rich, buttery flavor and high monounsaturated fat content. In Florida, nuts ripen from approximately July through November.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, oblong-lanceolate leaves, roughly 8 to 10 inches long, are arranged in whorls of three around the stem. Mature foliage on M. integrifolia has smooth or very slightly wavy margins — the integrifolia ('entire-leafed') name refers to this — though young leaves and seedlings can have more noticeably spiny edges. The dense, dark green canopy gives the tree a lush, almost tropical appearance year-round.

blocks.label: Propagation
blocks.text: Seed-grown trees are slow to bear — sometimes 10 or more years. Grafted trees on selected rootstocks typically begin producing nuts within 5 to 7 years, which is why most commercial and nursery-trade plants are grafted stock.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In winter or spring, look for the long, pendulous white flower racemes hanging from the branch tips — they are the most eye-catching feature and carry a sweet honey-like fragrance. In fall, scan the ground beneath the tree for the fallen nuts: green husks that have split open to reveal the smooth, dark brown inner shell. Pick one up and feel the weight — that density hints at how hard it is to crack. The leaves are also distinctive: glossy, oblong, and arranged in neat whorls of three, giving the canopy an orderly, layered texture.

seasonality.bloom_months: late winter to spring (typically January through April in Florida)
seasonality.bloom_description: Long, pendulous racemes of small, fragrant, creamy-white flowers hang from branch tips; honeybees are active visitors.
seasonality.notes: Nuts are not harvested from the tree — they are gathered from the ground after the husk splits and the nut falls naturally. A tree grown from seed can take a decade or more to first bear; grafted trees typically begin in 5 to 7 years and may take another few years to reach meaningful yields.

size.height: 30 to 40 feet in Florida landscapes (UF/IFAS); up to about 50 feet in the wild
size.habit: broad, dense, rounded to spreading evergreen canopy
size.growth_rate: slow to moderate; slow to begin bearing nuts

growing_conditions.light: full sun; does not tolerate shade
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: adaptable to sandy, loamy, and clay soils; well-drained is preferred; slightly acidic conditions preferred but tolerates a broader pH range
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: moderate to good once established; supplemental irrigation recommended during flowering and fruiting to prevent nut drop
growing_conditions.spacing: allow 20 to 25 feet from other trees and structures; commercial groves in Florida are planted at roughly 13 by 23 feet

other_notes: Macadamia integrifolia is not the only species you may encounter under the macadamia name. Its close relative Macadamia tetraphylla (rough-shell macadamia) and various hybrids such as 'Beaumont' are also grown in Florida and can look nearly identical in the landscape. The clearest difference is in the nut shell — M. integrifolia has a noticeably smooth, shiny shell, while M.
tetraphylla has a rougher, pebbled surface.

This tree is a member of the Proteaceae, one of the great plant families of the ancient Southern Hemisphere supercontinent Gondwana. Its distant relatives in that family include banksias, grevilleas, and proteas — plants that flourish across Australia, South Africa, and South America, and that share the family's characteristic showy flower structures.

species.behavior: Females nest alone or, less commonly, in small cooperative groups of mother and daughters — a rare flexibility that places this species in the middle ground between solitary and social bees. Nests are built inside any protected cavity: hollow wood, gaps in structures, or hollow stems, with each brood cell sealed using collected plant resins. Males spend much of their adult lives assembling a personal perfume by mopping aromatic oils from flowers, fungi, and resin-seeping wood with brush-like front tarsal pads, then transferring the compounds to spongy chambers inside their enlarged hind tibiae. They periodically display this blend — hovering and fanning — apparently to attract females. Both sexes are active foragers at flowers; females use buzz pollination on many plant species, rapidly vibrating their flight muscles against the flower to release pollen.

conservation.status: No conservation concern in Florida — the species is expanding its range and is locally common in south and central Florida. No threatened or protected status at any level. Its native Central American populations have not been formally assessed by IUCN under this species name.

danger.people: Females have a functional sting but are not aggressive and are unlikely to sting unless directly handled or trapped against skin. Males have no sting. Treat this bee as you would any solitary bee — give it space and it will ignore you entirely.
danger.pets: No meaningful danger to pets. A sting is possible if a pet snaps at or steps on one, but no different in effect from that of a common sweat bee.

species.diet: Adults of both sexes drink nectar from a wide range of flowering plants. Females additionally collect pollen to provision brood cells, often using buzz pollination — rapid vibration of flight muscles — to shake pollen free from flowers with pore-tipped anthers. In Florida, females gather much of their pollen from Senna and related plants; nectar is taken from many sources including Ruellia and Tecoma stans. Males collect no food from flowers — their flower visits are entirely for harvesting aromatic compounds for courtship. Females also gather plant resins, used as construction material to build and seal nest cells.
species.ecological_role: As an active forager across a broad range of plant families, this bee functions as a pollinator of both native and ornamental plants in Florida gardens and natural areas, including several species that benefit from buzz pollination. Females that collect resin from specialized resin-reward flowers may also facilitate pollination of those plants. On the other side of the ledger, research has shown that this bee can outperform native bees in pollinating certain invasive weeds, potentially helping those plants persist in disturbed habitats. Its own predators in Florida have not been specifically documented, but as a medium-sized insect it is presumably taken by insectivorous birds, spiders, and parasitoid wasps.
species.habitat: Gardens, parks, suburban yards, forest edges, and coastal habitats — essentially any landscape with flowering plants and potential nest cavities. In its native Central American range it favors dry forests, degraded woodlands, and disturbed areas rather than intact primary forest, and it has carried that adaptability into Florida. It has been recorded in urban neighborhoods, nature preserves, and Everglades National Park alike.

blocks.label: Size and build
blocks.text: Roughly the size of a honeybee but stockier, with a more rounded abdomen. Body length 10 to 13 mm (about half an inch); females run slightly larger than males.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Brilliant, uniform metallic green on the head, thorax, and abdomen. The iridescent sheen shifts slightly with angle and light — in direct sun this bee genuinely glints. Wings are dark and translucent.

blocks.label: The tongue
blocks.text: The proboscis is extraordinarily long — roughly two-thirds the length of the body — and is the single most reliable field mark distinguishing orchid bees from the similarly colored metallic sweat bees (family Halictidae), which have comparatively short tongues.

blocks.label: Males
blocks.text: Males have noticeably enlarged hind tibiae with a small external opening leading to a spongy internal chamber where fragrance compounds are stored. The front legs also bear brush-like tarsal pads used to mop up aromatic oils.

blocks.label: Females
blocks.text: Females carry pollen in corbiculae (pollen baskets) on the hind legs — visible as packed yellow or orange lumps when actively foraging. Females also possess a functional sting, though they rarely use it.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a compact, vivid metallic-green bee — the color alone is the first clue. At close range, the disproportionately long tongue, visible when the bee is feeding, confirms the ID and sets it apart from sweat bees. Foraging females show loaded pollen baskets on the hind legs; males hovering near fragrant flowers or wood are in perfume-collection mode, not feeding. Flight is rapid and buzzy, often hovering briefly at flowers.

interaction.guidance: Observe freely — these bees are intent on foraging and largely indifferent to people watching at close range. Avoid grabbing or trapping them. If you see a male hovering near a fragrant plant or weathered wood, watch quietly; the perfume-collecting behavior is unhurried and easy to observe.

invasive.notes: An introduced, non-native species that has naturalized across the southern half of Florida since 2003. It is an effective pollinator of many plants, including some invasive weeds, and its long-term ecological effects are still being studied. The park notes its presence as part of an honest picture of Florida's changing invertebrate community — fascinating to watch, but not a native.

more_information: The name dilemma has a story behind it. When researchers first caught this bee in Florida they thought they knew exactly what it was — Euglossa viridissima, a well-studied Central American orchid bee. It took years of work on mandible anatomy, perfume chemistry, and genetics to realize the Florida population was actually an undescribed sibling species.
The name dilemma, coined in 2011 by the scientists who formally described it, is a direct nod to that years-long taxonomic puzzle.

Female orchid bees are largely solitary by inclination but not by rule. A female typically builds and provisions her own nest alone, using plant resins she collects like a tiny bricklayer to construct and seal a series of brood cells inside any suitable cavity — a hollow in dead wood, a metal pipe leg, a gap in a wall.

Occasionally, daughters stay on at the natal nest and help their mother, producing small cooperative groups with a clear division of labor. This flexibility, sitting somewhere between solitary and fully social, makes Euglossa dilemma an active subject of research on how complex social behavior evolves.

The ecological picture in Florida is genuinely mixed. This bee pollinates a wide range of native and ornamental plants and has become a locally important pollinator in some habitats. At the same time, it has been shown to be a more effective pollinator of certain invasive weeds than native bees are, which can help those weeds spread.
That tension — a beautiful, charismatic insect that is also a non-native capable of unintended ecological effects — is exactly what makes it worth paying attention to.
quick_hits: That blazing metallic green is not a trick of the light — this bee's exoskeleton contains structural pigments that refract light the way a prism does, producing a color closer to polished jewelry than anything you expect from an insect.

Male orchid bees do not collect food when they visit flowers. Instead they gather aromatic compounds — from petals, fungi, and even rotting wood — and store them in hollow chambers in their hind legs to blend a species-specific perfume used only for courtship.

Despite the name, this bee does not need orchids. Florida has none of the perfume orchids it evolved with in Central America, yet the males improvise with basil, native wildflowers, and other scented plants — and the species has done just fine without them.

First detected in Broward County in 2003, likely tucked inside a wooden shipping pallet from Mexico, this bee has since spread on its own to at least 23 Florida counties — including Manatee County by 2017 — making it one of the more quietly successful non-native insects in the state.
species.range_and_origin: The Dilemma Orchid Bee is native to Central America and Mexico, where it inhabits dry forests, disturbed woodlands, pastures, parks, and gardens — habitats with less intact forest cover than most orchid bees require. It first appeared in Florida in 2003, when specimens were captured in Broward County fruit fly traps; researchers believe a nest arrived concealed inside a wooden shipping pallet from Mexico. By 2022 it had spread to 23 counties across south and central Florida, reaching Manatee County by 2017. It is an introduced, non-native species that has naturalized across the southern half of the peninsula.

seasonality.note: Active year-round in the warmest parts of its Florida range; at Manatee County's latitude, cold winter nights may suppress activity in December and January. No strongly defined peak season — look for it whenever flowering plants are available and midday temperatures are warm.

size.length: 10 to 13 mm (about 0.4 to 0.5 in); females slightly larger than males
size.lifespan: Not well documented for wild individuals; lifespan data omitted pending a reliable source

species.sounds: Essentially silent in the conventional sense — no calls or songs. In flight and during buzz pollination the bee produces an audible, rapid buzz from the vibration of its flight muscles, noticeably more intense during the sonication of flowers. That brief, high-pitched burst is often the first sign a bee is working a flower nearby.
species.when_to_see: Active year-round in the warmest parts of its Florida range, though at Manatee County's latitude cool winter nights may reduce activity temporarily. Most visible during warm, sunny mornings and afternoons. No strong single peak season — adults can be found in any month when temperatures are suitable and flowers are available.
species.where_to_look: Check any area with actively blooming flowers, particularly plants with tubular blossoms or those that require buzz pollination — pentas, Ruellia, Tecoma, and native thistles are documented Florida favorites. Males may be found hovering near fragrant flowers or sniffing at resin-seeping wood. Watch for the metallic flash of green as the bee hovers briefly before landing; it moves quickly but pauses long enough at each flower to observe.

quick_hits: It looks exactly like an American crow — slightly smaller, slightly glossier — and the only reliable way to tell is the voice: a short, nasal 'uh-uh' instead of the familiar 'caw.'
It is the crow of coastal and riverside Florida, far more common near water than its larger cousin.
An opportunist through and through — fish, garbage, eggs stolen from other birds' nests, fast food, whatever is available.
If you hear a crow near the ponds and it sounds a little nasally and uncertain, that is probably the fish crow.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern and mid-Atlantic United States. Year-round resident along the Florida coast and waterways.
more_information: The fish crow is one of those birds that exists in the shadow of a more famous relative. It is slightly smaller than the American crow and nearly identical in appearance, so most people never realize there are two species.
The voice is the key — a short, somewhat nasal, two-note call often described as 'uh-uh' or 'uh-oh,' lacking the full-throated 'caw' of the American crow.
Like all corvids it is intelligent and social, foraging in groups and quickly learning to exploit human food sources.

blocks.label: Voice
blocks.text: Short, nasal 'uh-uh' — distinctly different from the American crow's full 'caw.'

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Slightly smaller than an American crow, but the difference is subtle.

blocks.label: Habitat
blocks.text: Strongly tied to water — coast, rivers, ponds.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Often in small groups near water or open areas.

identification.what_to_look_for: A crow near water that gives a short, nasal 'uh-uh' instead of a full 'caw.' If you are near the coast or a pond in Florida and hear a crow, the odds favor fish crow.

species.diet: Fish, crustaceans, insects, eggs, fruit, garbage — a true generalist.
species.behavior: Forages on the ground, in shallow water, and in trees. Robs nests. Gathers in communal roosts.
species.sounds: A short, nasal 'uh-uh' or 'uh-oh' — the single best way to separate it from the American crow.
species.ecological_role: Scavenger and nest predator; part of the coastal food web.
species.plant_connections: Eats some fruit and berries; forages around many tree species.
species.habitat: Coastal areas, rivers, ponds, parking lots, and open ground near water.
species.where_to_look: Near the ponds or anywhere open — listen for the nasal call.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive.

quick_hits: A fast, dark butterfly with an iridescent blue-green body and two long tails trailing from its hindwings is a long-tailed skipper.
Skippers are the fighter jets of the butterfly world — small, stout, and quick, with a darting, hard-to-follow flight.
That blue-green shimmer on its body and wing bases catches the light beautifully when it pauses to nectar.
Its caterpillar has a second career as the "bean leafroller," folding and stitching bean and pea leaves into shelters — occasionally to a gardener's annoyance.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States and south through the American tropics. In Florida it is a common butterfly of gardens, field edges, and weedy open ground.
more_information: The long-tailed skipper belongs to the skipper family, a group of small, powerfully built butterflies named for their quick, skipping flight. This one stands out for the pair of long tails on its hindwings and the iridescent blue-green sheen across its furry body and inner wings.
Adults are eager flower-visitors, uncoiling a long proboscis to drink nectar and pollinating as they go. Their flight is fast and erratic, which makes them easy to overlook and hard to follow.

The caterpillar leads a different life. Known as the bean leafroller, it cuts and folds the leaves of beans, peas, and related legumes into rolled shelters held with silk, feeding from inside. In a vegetable garden it can be a minor pest, but it is the same insect that becomes the shimmering adult skipping among the flowers.

blocks.label: Body and wing bases
blocks.text: Iridescent blue-green over a dark brown ground color.

blocks.label: Hindwings
blocks.text: Each drawn out into a long tail.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Brown with darker bands.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast, low, and darting.

identification.what_to_look_for: A dark, quick butterfly with a blue-green sheen and two long hindwing tails, nectaring at flowers or zipping low over weedy ground. The long tails and shimmer separate it from other skippers.

species.diet: Adults sip nectar from many flowers. Caterpillars eat the leaves of beans, peas, and other legumes.
species.behavior: Active by day, with a fast, darting flight low over vegetation. Visits flowers frequently and basks with wings partly open. Caterpillars build rolled-leaf shelters on host plants.
species.ecological_role: A pollinator as an adult and, as a caterpillar, a leaf-feeder on legumes — part of the everyday garden food web and prey for birds and other insects.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are legumes — beans, peas, and wild relatives; adults nectar at a wide range of flowers.

size.lifespan: adults live a couple of weeks.

species.habitat: Gardens, field and woodland edges, weedy lots, and other open, flowery places, especially near legume plants.
species.where_to_look: Around the flower beds and weedy edges, nectaring or darting low. Watch for the long-tailed silhouette and blue-green flash.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season butterfly, common from spring through fall.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling its delicate wings; planting nectar flowers is the best way to attract it.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southeastern U.S. Not invasive (though its bean-leafroller caterpillar can be a minor garden nuisance).

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #17217419 (ktreffinger); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: Both flash blue-green, but the long-tailed skipper is smaller and trails two long hindwing tails; the mangrove skipper is larger, tail-less, with bolder neon-blue streaks.

quick_hits: A slender orange-and-black bug with long legs, prowling leaves and stems, is a milkweed assassin bug — a fierce hunter of other insects.
It is firmly on the gardener's side. It hunts down aphids, caterpillars, and many pest insects, helping keep gardens in balance.
It kills with a stab. A sharp curved beak injects venom that paralyzes prey and dissolves its insides, which the assassin bug then drinks.
A word of caution: that same beak can deliver a sharp, briefly painful jab if the bug is handled or trapped against skin.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the warmer Americas and common across the southern United States. In Florida it is widespread in gardens, fields, and brushy areas through the warm season.
more_information: The milkweed assassin bug is a small predator with an outsized appetite for pests. Bright orange and black with long, spindly legs, it stalks foliage in search of aphids, caterpillars, beetle larvae, and other soft-bodied insects, making it a welcome presence in most gardens.

Its hunting tool is a curved, needle-like beak (a rostrum). The bug grabs prey with its sticky front legs, drives in the beak, and injects saliva that both paralyzes the victim and liquefies its tissues, then drinks the meal — efficient, if grim.

The one caution is the same beak. This is a predator built to pierce, and a carelessly handled or pinned assassin bug can stab in self-defense, delivering a sharp, briefly painful jab. Watch it work, but let it be.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Bright reddish-orange and black, with long thin legs and antennae.

blocks.label: Beak
blocks.text: A short, curved piercing beak tucked under the head.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Slender and elongated, with a narrow "neck."

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Moves deliberately over leaves and stems, hunting.

identification.what_to_look_for: A leggy orange-and-black bug creeping over garden foliage, often near aphid clusters or other small insects. The slim build and curved beak mark it as an assassin bug rather than a harmless look-alike.

species.diet: Other insects — aphids, caterpillars, beetle larvae, flies, and various soft-bodied pests — caught and drained through its piercing beak.
species.behavior: Active by day, hunting slowly and patiently across foliage. Ambushes and grabs prey with sticky forelegs. Solitary and not aggressive toward people unless handled.
species.ecological_role: A beneficial predator that helps control aphids and other plant pests, a natural ally in keeping garden insect populations in check.
species.plant_connections: Hunts across garden and wild foliage of all kinds, drawn to plants where pest insects gather.

size.lifespan: adults live several weeks to a few months.

species.habitat: Gardens, field edges, and brushy areas with plenty of plants and insect prey.
species.where_to_look: On foliage throughout the garden beds, often near clusters of aphids or other small insects. Look closely and let it hunt undisturbed.
species.when_to_see: Most active in the warm season, spring through fall.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone, but a handled or trapped assassin bug can stab with its beak, delivering a sharp, briefly painful jab. Do not pick it up.
danger.pets: Not a real danger to pets, which generally leave it alone.

interaction.guidance: Look, do not touch. Enjoy watching it hunt, but do not handle it — that curved beak is for self-defense as well as prey.

invasive.notes: Native to the Americas and to Florida. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern. Common and widespread.
